title: Deep learning of the sectional appearances of 3D CT images for anatomical structure segmentation based on an FCN voting method
architecture_details: 2D FCN semantic segmentation (VGG16 downsampling with fully-connected layers converted to conv; skip connections; deconvolution upsampling variants FCN32s/16s/8s/4s/2s). 3D segmentation via 3D->2D sampling in axial/coronal/sagittal planes and 2D->3D voxel-wise majority voting fusion. Transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained VGG16; fine-tuning by progressively adding deconvolution layers.
loss_function: Sum of pixel-wise losses between prediction and ground-truth label map (implies softmax cross-entropy; not explicitly named).
preprocessing: No intensity normalization; CT (12-bit, 1-channel) linearly mapped to 8-bit grayscale (0–255) and duplicated into 3-channel RGB to match VGG16 input; uses full 2D slice as receptive field; multi-view slice extraction in 3 orthogonal directions.
postprocessing: Voxel-wise majority voting across three orthogonal 2D segmentations; tie-breaking by choosing label of organ type with largest volume (prior).
datasets: ['Computational Anatomy project shared dataset (Tokushima University Hospital)']
dataset_size: 240 annotated 3D CT scans from 200 patients (of 640 total scans in dataset); split typically 228 train / 12 test (repeated 3 times); additional 4-fold CV with 60 test folds
organs_segmented: ['Right lung', 'Left lung', 'Heart', 'Aorta', 'Esophagus', 'Liver', 'Gallbladder', 'Stomach and duodenum (2nd position/region)', 'Stomach and duodenum lumen', 'Contents inside stomach and duodenum', 'Spleen', 'Right kidney', 'Left kidney', 'Inferior vena cava', 'Portal vein / splenic vein / superior mesenteric vein region', 'Pancreas', 'Bladder', 'Prostate', 'Uterus']
other_metrics: {'IU/Jaccard_per_organ_test_mean': {'Right Lung': 0.916, 'Left Lung': 0.898, 'Heart': 0.839, 'Aorta': 0.628, 'Esophagus': 0.065, 'Liver': 0.88, 'Gallbladder': 0.478, 'Stomach and Duodenum (2nd_pos.)': 0.414, 'Stomach and Duodenum Lumen': 0.405, 'Contents inside of Stomach and Duodenum': 0.129, 'Spleen': 0.766, 'Right Kidney': 0.768, 'Left Kidney': 0.755, 'Inferior Vena Cava': 0.412, 'Portal/Splenic/Superior Mesenteric Vein region': 0.13, 'Pancreas': 0.36, 'Bladder': 0.543, 'Prostate': 0.305, 'Uterus': 0.207}, 'mean_voxel_accuracy_test': 0.879, 'frequency_weighted_IU_test': 0.805, 'mean_IU_after_voting_over_19_targets': 0.53, 'mean_IU_single_view_no_voting': {'axial': 0.491, 'coronal': 0.503, 'sagittal': 0.484}}
comparison_methods: ['Cascade U-Nets (two-stage 3D U-Net cascade) (from cited work)', 'Single 3D U-Net (ROI-sized input)', 'Conventional multi-organ segmentation method (Okada et al.)', 'SGD vs ADAM optimizer comparison', 'SegNet (preliminary replacement of FCN; not better)']
gpu_used: NVIDIA GeForce GTX TITAN X (12 GB)
training_time: Approximately 3 days
inference_time: 3 minutes 43 seconds per CT scan (512×512×221) (reported; truncated in provided text but time value given)
limitations: 3D FCN/3D U-Net on full volumes not feasible due to GPU memory limits; poorer performance for small-volume/tubular structures (e.g., esophagus, small vessels, gallbladder, pancreas) and limited image resolution; relies on transfer learning (training from scratch did not converge with available data).
future_work: Increase number of training CT scans to improve performance (noted for spleen/prostate/uterus drops under reduced training); potential customized architectures (e.g., SegNet variants) for better small-target segmentation (suggested indirectly).
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep-learning method for multi-organ segmentation in 3D CT using a 2D FCN applied to orthogonal slices with 3D majority-voting fusion; evaluated quantitatively (IU/Jaccard, voxel accuracy, frequency-weighted IU) on 240 annotated 3D CT scans across 19 anatomical structures.

title: AnatomyNet: Deep learning for fast and fully automated whole-volume segmentation of head and neck anatomy
architecture_details: AnatomyNet: modified 3D U-Net for whole-volume input with only one downsampling layer; replaces conv blocks with 3D squeeze-and-excitation (SE) residual blocks; skip connections use concatenation; outputs 10-class softmax (9 OARs + background).
loss_function: Hybrid Dice + focal loss with masked and class-weighted terms to handle missing annotations (a=b=0.5; k tuned, best reported k=0.5 for Dice+focal in experiments).
preprocessing: Data cleaning/curation: unify annotation names across sources; match annotations to CT; convert RT format to label masks; remove chest region to focus on head/neck; input is cropped whole-volume CT. Data augmentation: affine transformation and elastic deformation (on-the-fly).
postprocessing: None/minimal (paper states little pre- or postprocessing; no explicit postprocessing described).
datasets: ['MICCAI Head and Neck Auto Segmentation Challenge 2015 (train set)', 'TCIA Head-Neck Cetuximab collection', 'TCIA Quebec institutions head & neck RT dataset (4 institutions)', 'MICCAI Head and Neck Auto Segmentation Challenge 2015 (test set)']
dataset_size: Training: 261 CT volumes (38 + 46 + 177); Test: 10 CT volumes (MICCAI 2015 challenge test set).
organs_segmented: ['brain stem', 'optic chiasm', 'mandible', 'optic nerve (left)', 'optic nerve (right)', 'parotid gland (left)', 'parotid gland (right)', 'submandibular gland (left)', 'submandibular gland (right)']
dice_per_organ: {'brain stem': 0.8665, 'chiasm': 0.5322, 'mandible': 0.9251, 'optic nerve left': 0.721, 'optic nerve right': 0.7064, 'parotid left': 0.8807, 'parotid right': 0.8735, 'submandibular left': 0.8137, 'submandibular right': 0.813}
other_metrics: {'95%_Hausdorff_distance_mm': {'brain stem': '6.42 ± 2.38', 'chiasm': '5.76 ± 2.49', 'mandible': '6.28 ± 2.21', 'optic nerve left': '4.85 ± 2.32', 'optic nerve right': '4.77 ± 4.27', 'parotid left': '9.31 ± 3.32', 'parotid right': '10.08 ± 5.09', 'submandibular left': '7.01 ± 4.44', 'submandibular right': '6.02 ± 1.78'}}
comparison_methods: ['MICCAI 2015 challenge best results (various teams/atlas-based methods)', 'Fritscher et al. (atlas-based ConvNets)', 'Ren et al. (interleaved ConvNets with atlas/ROI)', 'Wang et al.']
inference_time: 0.12 s per CT volume (example size 178×302×225) for full segmentation
limitations: Does not explicitly model shape priors/connectivity; can produce small false positive outlier regions; translation/rotation invariance may lose spatial info. Evaluation mainly Dice; Hausdorff distance can be sensitive to outliers for whole-volume approach. Annotation inconsistencies across multi-source training data affect performance.
future_work: Incorporate shape/surface priors and multiresolution/deeper receptive fields to reduce outliers and improve surface metrics; identify clinically relevant evaluation metrics in consultation with physicians; address annotation inconsistencies across datasets.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel end-to-end 3D deep learning (modified 3D U-Net with SE residual blocks) for whole-volume head & neck CT organ-at-risk segmentation, evaluated quantitatively (Dice, 95% HD) on MICCAI 2015 test set with reported improvements and fast inference.

title: U-Net based deep learning bladder segmentation in CT urography
architecture_details: Modified 2D U-Net (5x5 convs, same padding, concatenation after two conv layers, sigmoid output) and 3D U-Net (3x3x3 convs, same padding, 192-slice input stack, sigmoid output). Outputs likelihood map then thresholding (0.2) + largest connected component.
loss_function: Binary cross-entropy (optimized with Adam)
preprocessing: Exclude slices without bladder using radiologist-marked first/last bladder slice; optional downsampling 512x512->256x256 via 2x2 box filter + stride 2; automated center cropping (256x256 from 512x512; 128x128 from 256x256). For 3D model, select fixed 192-slice stack centered between first/last bladder slice to fit GPU memory.
postprocessing: Threshold likelihood map at 0.2; keep largest connected component as bladder; generate contour from binary mask.
datasets: ['University of Michigan CT urography (CTU) dataset (institutional, 173 patients)']
dataset_size: 173 CTU cases (81 train/validation, 92 independent test); 7,629 bladder-containing slices in train/val and 8,553 in test
other_metrics: {'AVI': '93.4 ± 9.5% (best 2D U-DL, test set); 90.6 ± 11.9% (best 3D U-DL, test set)', 'AJI (Jaccard)': '85.0 ± 11.3% (best 2D U-DL, test set); 82.6 ± 14.2% (best 3D U-DL, test set)', 'AVE': '-4.2 ± 14.2% (best 2D U-DL, test set); -2.3 ± 21.7% (best 3D U-DL, test set)', 'AAVE': '9.2 ± 11.5% (best 2D U-DL, test set); 11.5 ± 18.5% (best 3D U-DL, test set)', 'AMD': '2.7 ± 2.5 mm (best 2D U-DL, test set); 3.1 ± 3.2 mm (best 3D U-DL, test set)', 'AHD': '9.7 ± 7.6 mm (best 2D U-DL, test set); 11.4 ± 10.0 mm (best 3D U-DL, test set)'}
comparison_methods: ['DCNN-LS (deep CNN + level sets within user-input bounding box) baseline', 'CLASS-LCR (level set based)', 'LS-HF (level sets using Haar feature-based likelihood map)', '2D U-DL vs 3D U-DL variants; different resolutions and cropping settings']
training_time: Per-epoch time examples: 2D U-DL 512x512 with cropping 18.5 min (without cropping 70.0 min); 3D U-DL 512x512 with cropping 32.5 min; 3D U-DL 256x256 with cropping 10 min (reported in Table IV).
limitations: 3D model constrained by GPU memory (cannot input full CTU; uses fixed 192-slice stack; 512x512x192 too large). Some test cases still suboptimal (2/92 with volume intersection ratio <50%; 3/92 between 50% and 80%). 3D U-DL underperformed 2D U-DL, possibly due to smaller effective training sample size for 3D volumes.
future_work: Not explicitly stated in provided text.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel U-Net-based deep learning method for bladder (organ) segmentation in CT urography, includes a 3D U-Net variant operating on 3D CT volumes, and reports quantitative evaluation (AVI/AJI, distance metrics) with comparisons to prior methods.

title: Automatic multi-organ segmentation in dual-energy CT (DECT) with dedicated 3D fully convolutional DECT networks
architecture_details: Four dedicated DECT fusion variants: (1) LC-net: trainable linear combination (alpha blending) of low/high energy images inside network; (2) MC-net: dual-energy as 2-channel input with separate kernels in first conv (low-level fusion); (3) MI-net: two-branch encoder (separate feature extraction per energy) with feature fusion at bottleneck and concatenations; (4) Late fusion: two independent U-Nets with trainable per-class linear fusion of outputs. Also evaluates residual-block versions (full pre-activation residual blocks with dimension matching; replaces conv layers in encoder depths). Patch-wise 3D training.
loss_function: Weighted multi-class Dice loss with median frequency balancing + boundary compensation term (k hyperparameter) (Eqs. 4–5).
preprocessing: Downsample volumes by factor 2; patch extraction 32×32×32 with overlap 16; data augmentation (translation, rotation, scaling, flipping, noise, elastic deformation).
postprocessing: Largest connected component filtering to remove misclassified voxels.
datasets: ['45 clinical thorax/abdomen dual-energy CT (DECT) datasets acquired on Siemens SOMATOM Definition Force (dual-source)']
dataset_size: 45 DECT volumes (5-fold cross-validation; per fold: 27 train / 9 val / 9 test)
other_metrics: {'AUC': {'right lung': 0.99, 'left lung': 0.99, 'liver': 0.99, 'spleen': 0.95, 'right kidney': 0.96, 'left kidney': 0.97}, 'Hausdorff_distance': {'right lung': 'best reported ~3.82 (MI-Res)', 'left lung': 'best reported ~4.01 (MI-Res)', 'liver': 'best reported ~6.49 (MC-Res) / ~6.60 (MI-Res) / ~6.67 (LC-Res)', 'spleen': 'best reported ~4.07 (LC-Res)', 'right kidney': 'best reported ~2.28 (Late fusion Res)', 'left kidney': 'best reported ~2.24 (Late fusion Res)'}}
comparison_methods: ['Late fusion vs LC-net vs MC-net vs MI-net (with/without residual blocks)', 'Single-energy 3D U-Net baselines using only low-energy image', 'Single-energy 3D U-Net baselines using only high-energy image']
training_time: Reported per architecture (example: MI-net ~115 h 15 min; MC-net ~94 h 8 min; LC-net ~103 h 20 min; Late fusion ~106 h 32 min; residual variants up to ~186 h 32 min).
limitations: Small dataset size (45 volumes) may limit learning; patch-wise training can introduce patch-edge artifacts and damage spatial relationships, especially for large organs; patch size constrained by GPU memory and affects accuracy/time; balance weight k in loss affects organs differently and requires tuning.
future_work: Investigate more organs; improve loss with dynamic weighting for k; address patch limitations via dilated convolutions and/or improved postprocessing.
screening_notes: Proposes novel dedicated 3D fully convolutional DECT networks (multiple fusion strategies + residual variant) for multi-organ segmentation on 3D CT volumes; evaluates on 45 DECT CT scans with quantitative metrics (Dice, Hausdorff, AUC) and compares architectures/baselines.

title: CT-based multi-organ segmentation using a 3D self-attention U-net network for pancreatic radiotherapy
architecture_details: 3D U-Net with attention gates on skip connections + deep supervision at multiple decoder stages; patch-based training (512×512×32) with sliding window (step 16); 3 down-sampling blocks and 3 up-sampling blocks; softmax multi-class (9 labels incl. background); patch fusion by averaging in overlap regions.
loss_function: Deep supervised compound loss: weighted cross-entropy + Dice loss (DSC-based) across decoder stages (ct = 0.8^(t-1), t=1..3; balance parameter b=0.5).
preprocessing: Patch extraction via sliding window along superior-inferior direction (512×512×32, step 16); data augmentation (rotation 0/30/60/90°, flip, scaling 1/1.25/1.5, affine warp) producing 72× augmented data.
postprocessing: Patch probability map fusion by averaging in overlapped regions; final segmentation by aggregating multi-organ probability maps; binary masks via thresholding/argmax from softmax probability maps (details of threshold not specified).
datasets: ['Institutional pancreatic radiotherapy CT simulation dataset (Emory University)']
dataset_size: 100 pancreatic cancer patients (CT simulation), 5-fold cross-validation (80 train / 20 test per fold)
other_metrics: {'sensitivity_mean': {'large bowel': 0.88, 'small bowel': 0.88, 'duodenum': 0.88, 'left kidney': 0.94, 'right kidney': 0.96, 'liver': 0.97, 'spinal cord': 0.87, 'stomach': 0.93}, 'specificity_mean': {'large bowel': 0.95, 'small bowel': 0.9, 'duodenum': 0.85, 'left kidney': 0.96, 'right kidney': 0.94, 'liver': 0.95, 'spinal cord': 0.88, 'stomach': 0.93}, 'HD95_mm_mean': {'large bowel': 4.94, 'small bowel': 6.97, 'duodenum': 4.17, 'left kidney': 3.42, 'right kidney': 2.97, 'liver': 4.38, 'spinal cord': 4.22, 'stomach': 6.4}, 'MSD_mm_mean': {'large bowel': 1.67, 'small bowel': 1.99, 'duodenum': 1.39, 'left kidney': 1.06, 'right kidney': 1.05, 'liver': 1.45, 'spinal cord': 1.07, 'stomach': 1.77}, 'RMSD_mm_mean': {'large bowel': 2.9, 'small bowel': 3.55, 'duodenum': 2.31, 'left kidney': 2.18, 'right kidney': 2.52, 'liver': 3.26, 'spinal cord': 2.05, 'stomach': 3.46}}
comparison_methods: ['U-Net (baseline)', 'U-Net with deep supervision (Unet-DS)', 'Unet-DS with compound loss (Unet-DS-CL)']
training_time: Not reported (training stopped after 200 epochs).
inference_time: < 1 s (for automatic generation of multi-organ contours)
limitations: Outliers due to CT image quality variations (contrast/no-contrast), noise/artifacts causing spurious labeling, and inconsistencies/missing manual contours at superior/inferior slices for large scan ranges; model may fail on some slices; human inspection still required for clinical use.
future_work: Extend to additional organs/VOIs (e.g., spleen, pancreas, tumor bed, vessels such as PV/SMA/CA); apply to CBCT-guided adaptive radiotherapy using corrected/synthetic CT from CBCT.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel 3D deep attention U-Net with deep supervision and compound loss for CT-based multi-organ (8 OARs) segmentation; uses 3D volumetric patches, reports multiple quantitative metrics (DSC, HD95, MSD, RMSD, sensitivity, specificity) with ablation study.

title: Complete Abdomen and Pelvis Segmentation using U-Net Variant Architecture
architecture_details: End-to-end 3D U-Net variant with pre-activation residual blocks in context modules (two 3×3×3 convs with dropout between), instance normalization, RReLU activations; downsampling via 3×3×3 stride-2 conv; upsampling via 2×2×2 nearest-neighbor interpolation followed by 3×3×3 conv; skip-connection concatenation; input volume 256×256×128.
loss_function: Weighted Dice loss (per-class weights inversely proportional to class prevalence); early stopping based on validation Dice
preprocessing: Resample CT volumes to 256×256×128 (cubic interpolation); HU windowing width 1000 HU, level 100 HU; rescale intensities to [0,1]. Ground-truth masks downsampled to 256×256×128 with nearest-neighbor interpolation for metrics.
postprocessing: None reported

title: General and custom deep learning autosegmentation models for organs in head and neck, abdomen, and male pelvis
architecture_details: Modified ResUnet3D with encoder-decoder, long skip connections and residual blocks (short-range residual connections). Added organ-based strategies: adaptive spatial resolution (ASR) for small/narrow organs and pseudo scan extension (PSE) for insufficient scan length.
loss_function: Weighted cross-entropy loss (optimized with Adam)
preprocessing: CT HU windowing/range clipping per site then linear normalization to [-1, 1]; HU ranges: MP [-400,400], HN [-1000,4000], ABD [-1000,4000]. Online random 3D elastic deformation augmentation.
postprocessing: Pseudo scan extension (PSE) to handle missing superior/inferior slices by repeating end slice with elastic contour transformations; ASR uses higher-resolution subregion processing for small organs.
datasets: ['MICCAI 2015 (HN subset, 41 cases)', 'Multi-institutional general datasets (MP, HN)', 'Single-institution custom datasets (MP, HN, ABD)']
dataset_size: Training: MP general 204, MP custom 50; HN general 65 (41 MICCAI2015 + 24 multi-institution), HN custom 47; ABD custom 58. Testing: G-MP 50, C-MP 10, G-HN 50, C-HN 10, C-ABD 13.
organs_segmented: ['Male pelvis: bladder, prostate, rectum, seminal vesicles, penile bulb, sigmoid, femur L/R, pelvis bone L/R, patient external', 'Head & neck: brain, brainstem, mandible, esophagus, eyes L/R, inner ears L/R, larynx, lenses L/R, optic nerves L/R, parotids L/R, submandibular glands L/R, spinal cord, patient external', 'Abdomen: aorta, large bowel, small bowel, duodenum, kidneys L/R, liver, pancreas, spinal cord, spleen, stomach, patient external']
comparison_methods: ['Chen et al. WBNet (site-specific networks)', 'Isensee et al. nnU-Net', 'Other recent published DL autosegmentation results (combined datasets referenced in paper)']
framework: Not specified (integrated for execution in ADMIRE, Elekta Inc.)
training_time: Not reported (trained 200–400 epochs)
inference_time: Approx. 30 s (MP), 120 s (HN), 70 s (ABD) for all OARs per case (on Intel Xeon Gold 6132 CPU @2.6 GHz, 128 GB RAM)
limitations: Lower performance for challenging/low-contrast or highly variable organs (e.g., penile bulb, submandibular glands, pancreas, bowels/duodenum) and cases with artifacts/complex anatomy; bowel and duodenum MDA often >2 mm; performance depends on scan length variability and training data representation.
future_work: Not explicitly stated; implied need for improved handling of difficult organs (e.g., pancreas/bowels), broader/representative training data, and further refinement for clinical robustness.
screening_notes: Presents a modified 3D ResUnet3D (3D U-Net) deep learning method for multi-organ (OAR) segmentation on CT across head & neck, abdomen, and male pelvis; uses 3D volumetric processing, reports quantitative metrics (DSC, MDA) and time efficiency, and introduces organ-based improvements (adaptive spatial resolution, pseudo scan extension).

title: 3D U-JAPA-Net: Mixture of Convolutional Networks for Abdominal Multi-organ CT Segmentation
architecture_details: Two-stage training: (1) a multi-class 3D U-Net (9 classes: 8 organs + background) trained on tiles; (2) weights transferred to 8 organ-specific expert 3D U-Nets (binary: organ vs background) trained on organ-focused regions determined by probabilistic atlas (PA). Inference: PA provides rough organ locations; expert networks applied to PA-nonzero regions; final largest connected component selection to remove debris. Uses BN before ReLU as in 3D U-Net reference.
loss_function: Not specified in provided text (trained by gradient descent; likely standard segmentation loss but not stated).
preprocessing: 2D U-Net: downsample slices to 256x256. 3D methods: use 132x132x116 voxel input tiles; outputs 44x44x28 due to valid convolutions/truncation. Test/train tiles arranged to avoid overlap to reduce computation. Probabilistic atlas constructed from training data (per Okada et al. 2015) and used to focus regions.
postprocessing: Largest connected component selection to remove debris (applied to all U-Net-based systems).
datasets: ['In-house abdominal CT dataset (late arterial phase, normal organs)']
dataset_size: 47 CT scans from 47 patients; 2-fold cross-validation (also reports 4-fold CV for proposed method).
other_metrics: {'recall_per_organ': {'liver': 0.978, 'spleen': 0.966, 'right kidney': 0.969, 'left kidney': 0.981, 'pancreas': 0.816, 'gallbladder': 0.906, 'aorta': 0.964, 'inferior vena cava': 0.903}, 'precision_per_organ': {'liver': 0.964, 'spleen': 0.973, 'right kidney': 0.99, 'left kidney': 0.987, 'pancreas': 0.922, 'gallbladder': 0.937, 'aorta': 0.975, 'inferior vena cava': 0.92}, 'dice_4fold_cv_pancreas': 0.882}
comparison_methods: ['S-CSL-UI (Okada et al. 2015 prior-based method)', '2D U-Net', '3D U-Net', 'Mixture of 3D U-Nets without transfer learning (3D M-U-nets)', 'Roth et al. hierarchical 3D FCN (reported comparison table for liver/spleen/pancreas)']
training_time: 2D U-Net: 2.5 h; 3D U-Net (all organs): 16 h; expert 3D U-Nets per organ: 40–130 min each except liver 9.5 h (epochs=30, lr=1e-4, batch size=3).
inference_time: Not reported.
limitations: Limited dataset size (47 cases); PA used only as non-zero mask due to small sample size/discrete probabilities; suggests need for larger dataset to increase reliability.
future_work: Train with larger dataset to improve performance; use probabilistic atlas values arithmetically (not just non-zero masking) when more samples make probabilities reliable.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep-learning-based 3D multi-organ CT segmentation method (3D U-JAPA-net) using 3D U-Net experts with transfer learning and probabilistic atlas guidance; evaluates on 47 CT volumes with quantitative Dice/precision/recall and comparisons to 2D/3D U-Net and prior methods.

title: Automated segmentation of kidney and renal mass and automated detection of renal mass in CT urography using 3D U-Net-based deep convolutional neural network
architecture_details: Two-stage cascaded 3D U-Net for kidney segmentation (low-resolution global localization then high-resolution local fine segmentation); separate renal mass model using a single 3D U-Net with multi-label learning for kidney/tumor/cyst; detection via maximum 3D VOI volume thresholding with ROC/AUC evaluation.
loss_function: Not reported in provided text (see Supplementary Material/Table S2 referenced).
preprocessing: Cropping to separate left/right kidneys; low-resolution global processing for localization; cropping to localized ROI; linear resampling of cropped images for fine segmentation.
postprocessing: Detection postprocessing: use maximum 3D VOI volume of predicted tumor/cyst per kidney and apply an optimal threshold (Youden index) for classification; other segmentation postprocessing not specified.
datasets: ['Single-institution CT urography (corticomedullary phase) dataset (441 patients)', 'Additional small renal tumor test set (35 patients)']
dataset_size: 441 patients (882 kidneys) split into train/val/test: 309/66/66 patients (618/132/132 kidneys); additional small tumor group: 35 patients (70 kidneys; tumors annotated only).
comparison_methods: ['No direct experimental comparison; discussion compares to prior 3D U-Net/KiTS19 results']
training_time: Not reported
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Only corticomedullary phase CTU studied (other phases not included); unbalanced renal mass size distribution (wide diameter range); only 3D U-Net evaluated without comparison to other network architectures; moderate segmentation accuracy for very small tumors/cysts and notable false positives.
future_work: Implied: extend to other CTU phases; address data imbalance/small lesion segmentation; compare with other network architectures and reduce false positives.
screening_notes: Primary method is a 3D U-Net-based deep CNN for automated kidney and renal mass segmentation on CT urography volumes; uses 3D context (3D U-Net, VOI-based detection), reports quantitative metrics (DSC, AUC, sensitivity/specificity/accuracy), and presents a cascaded 3D U-Net pipeline and detection strategy.

title: An application of cascaded 3D fully convolutional networks for medical image segmentation
architecture_details: Two-stage coarse-to-fine cascaded 3D U-Net/FCN. Stage 1 uses body mask candidate region C1 (~40% voxels). Stage 2 uses candidate region C2 derived from Stage-1 predictions dilated in 3D (radius r=3) to reduce to ~10% voxels. 3D U-Net with 4 resolution levels, skip connections, 3x3x3 convs, 2x2x2 pooling/up-conv, 1x1x1 final conv; batch normalization throughout; tiling/sliding-window inference with optional overlapping tiles (averaging probabilities). Input subvolume 132x132x116, output tile 44x44x28.
loss_function: Weighted voxel-wise softmax cross-entropy (class weights based on voxel counts within candidate region); SGD optimization
preprocessing: Downsample CT by factor 2 for training/inference then resample predictions back; Stage-1 candidate region via thresholding + morphological operations (hole filling, largest component) to obtain body mask; data augmentation with random 3D elastic deformations (max disp ~±4, grid spacing 32), random rotations (-5° to +5°), translations (-20 to 20 voxels).
postprocessing: No post-processing on FCN outputs; Stage-2 candidate region generation uses argmax foreground labels from Stage-1 then 3D dilation (r=3) to form C2; overlapping-tile probability averaging during inference (optional).
datasets: ['In-house Nagoya University Hospital abdominal CT (331 cases; 281 train/50 val; 7 labels)', 'Unseen external hospital test set (150 cases; liver/spleen/pancreas)', '240-case 18-class abdominal CT dataset used for direct comparison to 2D FCN voting method (Zhou et al.)', 'TCIA Pancreas-CT (82 cases; 4-fold CV)', 'Visceral Challenge dataset (20 cases; 20 labels) for fine-tuning', 'Unseen whole torso test set (10 cases; 8 labels) for transfer learning evaluation']
dataset_size: 331 CT (281 train/50 val) + 150 external test; additional experiments: 240 CT (18 classes), TCIA 82 CT, Visceral 20 CT, torso test 10 CT
other_metrics: {'reported_dispersion': 'Mean±Std Dice reported for multiple experiments; sensitivity/false-positive-rate curves for candidate region dilation radius selection (recall >99% and FPR ~10% at r=3).'}
comparison_methods: ['2D FCN orthogonal planes + majority voting (Zhou et al. 2016a/2017) on 240-case 18-class dataset', 'Atlas/registration/ML baselines on in-house pancreas dataset: Tong et al. (global affine + ML), Wang et al. (global affine), Wolz et al. (local non-rigid), Oda et al. (RF + graph cut)', '2D FCN pancreas methods on TCIA: Roth et al. (2017), Zhou et al. (2016b)']
gpu_used: NVIDIA GeForce GTX TITAN X (12 GB)
training_time: 2–3 days for 200k iterations on 281 cases (stage 1); stage 2 trained 115k iterations (time not separately stated)
inference_time: Per volume: 1.4–3.3 min per stage; 1.6–4.4 min with overlapping tiles in stage 2
limitations: 3D FCN requires tiling due to GPU memory limits; performance for some larger organs comparable to 2D FCNs; transfer learning pancreas performance low on unseen torso dataset due to contrast enhancement variation and high pancreas variability; direct comparisons across papers/datasets are difficult.
future_work: Explore alternative loss functions (e.g., Dice loss) to address class imbalance; try alternative optimizers (e.g., Adam); combine predictions from different models (ensembles); incorporate anatomical/topological constraints; hybrid 2D+3D multi-scale FCN approaches; reduce need for overlapping tiles as GPU memory increases and enable arbitrary-sized 3D inputs.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel cascaded coarse-to-fine two-stage 3D FCN (3D U-Net) for multi-organ segmentation on 3D CT volumes, with quantitative evaluation (Dice) on multiple CT datasets and comparisons; code is available.

title: MSS U-Net: 3D segmentation of kidneys and tumors from CT images with a multi-scale supervised U-Net
architecture_details: Multi-scale supervised 3D U-Net (deep supervision on decoder outputs); based on nnU-Net-style 3D U-Net; strided convolutions for downsampling (instead of pooling), transposed convolutions for upsampling (instead of trilinear interpolation), instance normalization; 6 levels (incl. bottleneck), base channels=30; patch-based training/inference with overlap and test-time augmentation.
loss_function: Multi-scale weighted sum of (Cross-Entropy + exponential logarithmic Soft Dice) with class weights; exponential logarithmic loss applied to Soft Dice with kidney/tumor weighting (0.4/0.6) and exponent 0.3; CE class weights: background 0.28, kidney 0.28, tumor 0.44; layer loss weights top-down: 0.4, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1.
preprocessing: Intensity clipping to [0.5th, 99.5th] percentiles to reduce artifacts/outliers; normalization using foreground mean/std; resampling to unify voxel spacing/affine (uses KiTS19 interpolated dataset).
postprocessing: 3D connected-component based filtering: keep 1–2 largest kidney components; keep tumor components attached to kidney; remove disconnected false positives.
datasets: ['KiTS19']
dataset_size: 210 CT scans total; split: 134 train / 34 val / 42 test (internal). Also reports KiTS19 challenge test set of 90 patients for leaderboard evaluation.
comparison_methods: ['Classic 3D U-Net (their implementation)', '2D PSPNet (ROI) (from Yang et al. 2018, cited)', '3D U-Net (ROI) (from Yang et al. 2018, cited)', '3D FCN-PPM (ROI) (from Yang et al. 2018, cited)', 'KiTS19 challenge top methods (e.g., residual 3D U-Net, multi-stage approaches) (leaderboard comparison)']
gpu_used: 2x Nvidia Tesla 32 GB GPUs (model not fully specified; 'Nvidia Tesla 32 GB')
training_time: Approximately 5 days (until convergence).
limitations: May miss very small tumors (reports cases with very low/zero tumor Dice); fixed receptive field of standard convolution may limit small/variable tumor detection; potential domain shift issues when applying to CTs from different vendors/settings (train/test split from same dataset distribution).
future_work: Investigate solutions for small tumor segmentation; consider deformable convolution to address fixed receptive field; improve generalization to different clinical CT distributions/vendors.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel multi-scale supervised 3D U-Net (MSS U-Net) for kidney organ segmentation (and tumor) from 3D CT volumes, with quantitative evaluation (Dice, Jaccard, Hausdorff, etc.) on KiTS19 and comparisons to baselines/state-of-the-art; includes preprocessing and postprocessing contributions.

title: Dual-energy CT for automatic organs-at-risk segmentation in brain-tumor patients using a multi-atlas and deep-learning approach
architecture_details: Two-step 3D U-Net applied in succession: (1) detect 3D bounding box per OAR, (2) per-OAR foreground segmentation within fixed-size bounding box; 3 encoding + 3 decoding layers; dropout=0.5; second stage trains one network per OAR; total 28 neural networks; implemented in TensorFlow (Python 2.7) using cuDNN.
loss_function: Weighted cross entropy
preprocessing: CT HU window/normalization: clamp/normalize between -500 and 1500 HU; resample/resize to unified voxel spacing 0.98×0.98×2.00 mm^3; for step-1 training: downsample volumes to half resolution and crop 48 voxels to remove air; eye lens handled by subtracting lens from eye mask before downsampling (no dedicated lens model due to small size).
postprocessing: Morphological cleanup of segmented OAR foreground: remove non-contiguous regions and fill binary holes; eye lens obtained by detecting hole within eye segmentation and labeling as lens.
datasets: ['Private DECT brain-tumor radiotherapy planning cohort (pseudo-monoenergetic CT derived from 80/140 kVp DECT)']
dataset_size: 14 primary brain-tumor patients (leave-one-out cross-validation; DL trained on N-1=13 each fold)
other_metrics: {'metrics_reported': ['95th percentile Hausdorff distance (HD95)', 'center of mass displacement (ΔCoM)'], 'note': 'HD95 and ΔCoM are reported in figures; specific numeric values not fully provided in the supplied text excerpt.'}
comparison_methods: ['In-house 3D multi-atlas segmentation (Elastix deformable registration + STAPLE consensus + morphological smoothing)', 'Literature comparison: Plastimatch MABS (Zaffino et al., values extracted from plots)']
gpu_used: NVIDIA GeForce Titan Xp; NVIDIA Quadro P6000
training_time: 53 ± 4 hours per neural network (leave-one-out setting)
inference_time: 20 ± 2 seconds per patient to segment all OARs (DL); MA ~10 minutes per patient on 400-core CPU cluster
limitations: Small cohort (N=14); DL trained on only 13 patients per fold; DL applied only to PMI-40 and PMI-70 due to GPU time constraints; difficulty segmenting small/complex structures (notably optic nerves; eye lens metrics unstable due to very small volume).
future_work: Evaluate on larger datasets and other body regions; prospective comparison between conventional 120 kVp SECT and DECT-derived PMIs; further improvements for challenging OARs (e.g., optic nerves) and robustness across PMI energies.
screening_notes: Uses a two-step 3D U-Net deep-learning approach for automatic organs-at-risk segmentation on dual-energy CT-derived pseudo-monoenergetic CT volumes; reports quantitative metrics (DSC, 95% HD, ΔCoM) and compares against a multi-atlas method; includes methodological details and evaluation on 3D volumes.

title: Abdominal multi-organ auto-segmentation using 3D-patch-based deep convolutional neural network
architecture_details: 3D patch-based U-Net (input patches 64×64×64) for multi-label semantic segmentation; trained with random patch sampling; combined with graph-cut algorithm post-processing to remove false positives and refine boundaries.
loss_function: Cross-entropy loss
preprocessing: Resampled/interpolated CT to 2 mm isotropic resolution (x,y,z); intensity normalization (mentioned as part of preprocessing).
postprocessing: Graph-cut algorithm refinement (data penalty + boundary term based on intensity discontinuity/gradient) to remove false positives and refine organ boundaries.
datasets: ['Asan Medical Center internal radiotherapy CT simulation dataset (hepatocellular carcinoma patients; 4D CT end-expiratory phase)']
dataset_size: 120 CT simulation scans total (train=80, val=20, test=20)
comparison_methods: ['Atlas-based auto-segmentation (Mirada Medical; atlas liver library of 20 CT simulation scans)', 'Inter-observer manual segmentation (resident vs radiographers/ground truth)']
training_time: 3–4 days (105 iterations) on single RTX Titan
limitations: Lower performance for stomach and duodenum (low intensity gradients, higher inter-observer variability); limited to upper-abdomen organs (not whole body); ground truth based on expert manual contours (human variability); time-efficiency evaluated mainly via time reduction; dataset may lack enough outlier cases (e.g., extensive liver expansion, insufficient kidney enhancement).
future_work: Improve stomach/duodenum via multi-resolution, multi-planar fusion, or semi-/partially-supervised approaches; extend to whole-body organ segmentation; further evaluate against more robust/true reference standards and assess additional clinical usefulness factors beyond time.
screening_notes: Proposes a 3D patch-based U-Net for abdominal multi-organ segmentation on CT volumes with graph-cut post-processing; evaluates on held-out test set with Dice, Hausdorff distance, and mean surface distance; includes methodological contribution beyond applying an off-the-shelf model.

title: Automated pancreas segmentation and volumetry using deep neural network on computed tomography
architecture_details: Four 3D U-Net-based models with skip connections + batch normalization; variants differ by blocks: basic 3D U-Net, dense U-Net, residual U-Net, residual dense U-Net. Uses 3×3×3 convs, ReLU, and simple 3D upsampling (not transposed conv). Four resolution steps.
loss_function: Dice loss (optimized with Adam, lr=0.001)
preprocessing: Resample/unify voxel spacing to (z,y,x)=(3mm,1mm,1mm); reconstruct CT and mask volumes; crop pancreas ROI using margin based on reconstructed mask (manual delineation used for cropping); resize via bilinear interpolation to (64,128,256,1); windowing (WL=60, WW=400); CLAHE contrast enhancement; restore to raw voxel spacing for evaluation; 4-fold cross-validation.
postprocessing: Binary thresholding of network probability output at 0.5; outputs restored to raw voxel spacing for evaluation.
datasets: ['Internal Gil Medical Center abdominal CT dataset', 'TCIA Pancreas-CT (NIH) dataset']
dataset_size: Internal: 1006 CT scans/patients (2016–2019); External: 82 CT scans (TCIA/NIH Pancreas-CT)
comparison_methods: ['Basic 3D U-Net', 'Dense 3D U-Net', 'Residual 3D U-Net']
limitations: Semi-automated pipeline: pancreas ROI cropping performed using manual delineation due to RAM/GPU memory limits; external performance lower likely due to different slice thickness; blurry pancreas boundaries can reduce accuracy; internal dataset not publicly shareable.
future_work: Develop fully automated pancreas segmentation (e.g., two-stage cascaded/coarse-to-fine networks) and explore other state-of-the-art segmentation strategies; validate on more diverse populations (races/ages/pancreatic volumes) and potentially other modalities.
screening_notes: Proposes and evaluates 3D U-Net-based deep learning variants (basic/dense/residual/residual-dense) for pancreas organ segmentation on CT volumes with quantitative metrics (DSC/precision/recall) and external validation (TCIA/NIH). Novel contribution includes large-scale dataset (n=1006) and residual-dense 3D U-Net comparison.

title: Fully automated organ segmentation in male pelvic CT images
architecture_details: Two-stage pipeline: (1) 5-channel multi-organ 2D U-Net for organ volume localization on downsampled full CT; (2) 3D U-Net (Cicek et al. 3D U-Net style) for segmentation with ResNeXt blocks in the encoder (aggregated residual transformations; 3x3x3 convs; maxpool stride 2x2x2; decoder with upsampling stride 2x2x2; ReLU; sigmoid output). Fixed slice counts around predicted volume: 32 slices for prostate, 64 for each OAR.
loss_function: Localization: custom organ-weighted Dice loss (DSCw). Segmentation: boundary-weighted Dice loss (DSCbw). Comparison also reported with weighted binary cross-entropy (BCE).
preprocessing: Crop out upper and lower 15% of CT volume (speed/memory); downsize images before localization; use localization output to crop/extract organ volumes; select fixed number of slices around predicted organ volume (32 prostate, 64 OARs).
postprocessing: Not explicitly described (workflow includes volume cropping based on localization; no additional morphological/CRF postprocessing reported).
datasets: ['UT Southwestern (UTSW) internal pelvic CT dataset']
dataset_size: 136 prostate cancer patients CT; 80% train / 20% test (test set 27 patients); training uses 4-fold leave-one-out style cross-validation and model averaging
comparison_methods: ['Weighted BCE loss vs weighted Dice loss (significance tests reported)', '2D U-Net', '3D U-Net', '3D U-Net with 1x1 convolutions', '3D U-Net with DenseNet', '3D U-Net with ResNet', 'Prior published methods: Shao et al. (2014), Gao et al. (2012), Gao et al. (2016), Feng et al. (2010), Martinez et al. (2014), Ma et al. (2017)']
training_time: Reported for prostate architecture comparison: ~1.9 hours (3D U-Net with ResNeXt blocks); other variants 2.0–2.8 hours
inference_time: Segmentation for test set (27 patients) 6–8 seconds (for compared networks); full workflow per patient (2D localization + cropping + 3D segmentation) takes a few minutes
limitations: Manual contours are imperfect ground truth due to inter-observer variability; rectum performance lower and more variable due to large anatomical variation and presence of rectal balloon inserts; need larger datasets to improve accuracy.
future_work: Use multiple observers and average contours as ground truth; create an ensemble network to combine organ segmentations without overlap; improve accuracy with larger datasets; adapt workflow to other organs.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep-learning workflow for pelvic CT multi-organ segmentation: 2D U-Net for localization followed by a 3D U-Net with ResNeXt blocks for volumetric segmentation. Uses CT volumes (136 patients) and reports quantitative metrics (Dice, ASD, Hausdorff) with comparisons to prior methods and ablations.

title: Interactive 3D U-net for the segmentation of the pancreas in computed tomography scans
architecture_details: Interactive 3D U-Net (iUnet): baseline 3D U-Net trained for best initial segmentation; interactive phase performs image-specific fine-tuning by freezing most layers and retraining selected layers (last layer + an additional deep/latent layer) using user scribbles and a voxel-weighted Dice-based loss with a volume-difference regularization term.
loss_function: Baseline: differentiable Dice loss (Milletari/V-Net style). Interactive: weighted Dice loss using scribble-derived weight map + volume difference penalty (lambda term).
preprocessing: Gaussian smoothing (sigma=0.75) before resampling; HU windowing -160 to 240 then rescale to [-1,1] with clipping; crop to bounding box derived from reference segmentation with 5% margin; resample to fixed size 64×64×24 using trilinear interpolation.
postprocessing: None explicitly reported (emphasizes avoiding organ-specific post-processing used in iFCN).
datasets: ['TCIA Pancreas-CT', 'BTCV Abdomen', 'Radboud UMC clinical CT cohort (late venous phase, 2015; subset used for validation)']
dataset_size: D1: 90 CT scans (public; from TCIA Pancreas-CT + BTCV). D2: 10 CT scans sampled from a 1905-scan Radboud UMC cohort for interactive validation.
other_metrics: {'time_manual': '15 min (reported in abstract; also shown as boxplot in seconds)', 'time_interactive_iUnet': '8 min (reported in abstract; median 48.4% time reduction vs manual)', 'statistical_tests': 'Wilcoxon signed-rank tests reported (e.g., p=0.017 manual vs interactive DSC; p<0.001 for time reduction; p<0.001 for initial vs refined).', 'inter_observer_dice_manual': 'Average 0.86 (95% CI 0.85–0.87)'}
comparison_methods: ['3D U-Net (Cicek et al.)', 'Dilated FCN / iFCN (Wang et al. interactive framework)']
gpu_used: NVIDIA RTX 2070 (baseline training); NVIDIA GTX 1080 (interactive training)
training_time: Baseline iUNet: ~12 hours for 2000 iterations (batch size 8).
inference_time: Not reported (interactive segmentation time reported instead).
limitations: Preprocessing is substantial (cropping uses ground-truth-derived bounding box; fixed low-resolution resampling to 64×64×24); performance affected by low contrast/complex anatomy (e.g., uncinate process) and low-resolution/partial volume artifacts; interactive fine-tuning can diverge/under-segment if too many layers are retrained; validation clinical data not publicly available.
future_work: Define clinically acceptable segmentation accuracy per use-case; leverage larger training sets for better generalization; potentially pretrain parts of the network (e.g., decoder) on large similar datasets; explore probabilistic/latent-space regularization; use higher-resolution/whole-volume processing with more GPU memory; add richer label/feature channels; replace ground-truth bounding boxes with user-provided or automatic detection.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel interactive 3D U-Net (iUnet) for pancreas organ segmentation in CT volumes, uses 3D convolutions, evaluates with Dice (and time) on public + clinical CT datasets, and compares against iFCN and 3D U-Net baselines.

title: Automatic Segmentation Using a Hybrid Dense Network Integrated With an 3D-Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling Module for Computed Tomography (CT) Imaging
architecture_details: Proposed 3D-ASPPDN: 3D DenseNet-based encoder–decoder with skip connections; 3D-ASPP module at the bottleneck using parallel atrous 3D convolutions (3×3×3) with dilation rates 3, 5, 7 plus a 1×1×1 conv branch and a global average pooling branch; concatenation followed by 1×1×1 conv; PReLU activations and batch normalization; downsampling via 2×2×2 stride-2 conv; upsampling via deconvolution.
loss_function: Dice coefficient loss (Dice-based objective)
preprocessing: Downsample in-plane resolution to 256×256; select slices with lesion/masks and include 5 additional empty slices at start/end of each volume (as described); train/val/test split of SegTHOR (32/8/20 cases).
postprocessing: None (explicitly states no postprocessing required).
datasets: ['SegTHOR 2019 (Thoracic Organs at Risk)']
dataset_size: SegTHOR: 40 training cases, 20 test cases; used 32 train / 8 val / 20 test
other_metrics: {'Jaccard': 'reported (values not provided in excerpt)', 'Sensitivity': 'reported (values not provided in excerpt)', 'Specificity': 'reported (values not provided in excerpt)', 'VOE': 'reported (values not provided in excerpt)', 'RVD': 'reported (values not provided in excerpt)', 'ASSD': 'reported (values not provided in excerpt)', 'Hausdorff/MSD': 'reported (values not provided in excerpt)'}
comparison_methods: ['3D V-Net (reimplemented)', '3D Attention U-Net (reimplemented)', 'Other SegTHOR 2019 state-of-the-art methods (various, referenced in Table 1)']
limitations: Notes typical 3D DL limitations: computational/memory constraints, risk of overfitting with limited training data, and challenges from low contrast/ambiguous boundaries and heterogeneous organ appearance; mentions small organs can be harder to segment.
future_work: Explore attention mechanisms and incorporate different parameters to improve generalization to other medical datasets.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep learning 3D volumetric CT organ segmentation network (3D DenseNet encoder-decoder + 3D atrous spatial pyramid pooling). Uses 3D convolutions on CT volumes (SegTHOR 2019) to segment multiple thoracic organs-at-risk and reports quantitative metrics including Dice and others with comparisons to prior methods.

title: Research on Liver Segmentation of CT Image Based on Deep Active Contour Model
venue: 2022 3rd International Conference on Computer Vision, Image and Deep Learning & International Conference on Computer Engineering and Applications (CVIDL & ICCEA)
architecture_details: Two-stage 3DAC: (1) two-branch 3D V-Net (encoder-decoder with skip connections) outputs (a) liver probability map used for global parameter mapping (lambda1/lambda2) and (b) regressed signed distance map (SDM) used as initialization level set; (2) 3D Chan–Vese active contour model refines the liver surface using CNN-derived parameter maps and SDM initialization; narrow-band level set used for efficiency.
loss_function: Dice loss + L2 distance loss on signed distance map regression (multi-task training)
preprocessing: Crop in x/y/z with liver region centered; HU truncation to [-100, 400]; normalization; histogram equalization; data augmentation: random flipping and rotations (90/180/270) along axial plane; training uses random cropped 128×128×80 sub-volumes; sliding-window inference to reconstruct full volume.
postprocessing: 3D Chan–Vese active contour refinement (level set evolution) as second-stage surface smoothing/refinement; narrow-band level set method.
datasets: ['AbdomenCT-1K']
dataset_size: Used 220 CT scans from AbdomenCT-1K (200 train, 20 evaluation/validation); AbdomenCT-1K total 1112 scans from 12 centers (not all used).
comparison_methods: ['Manual initialization active contour model (ACM/CV)', 'U-Net', 'V-Net', 'DALS']
limitations: Uses only the first 220 scans from AbdomenCT-1K due to GPU memory constraints; focuses on liver segmentation despite dataset containing multiple organs.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel two-stage 3D deep active contour framework (3DAC) combining a 3D V-Net backbone with a 3D Chan–Vese active contour refinement for liver segmentation from 3D CT volumes; evaluated quantitatively on AbdomenCT-1K with Dice/Jaccard/ASSD/95HD and comparisons to U-Net, V-Net, DALS, and ACM.

title: Hybrid 3D-ResNet Deep Learning Model for Automatic Segmentation of Thoracic Organs at Risk in CT Images
venue: International Conference on Industrial Engineering, Applications and Manufacturing (ICIEAM)
architecture_details: Hybrid 3D-ResNet encoder-decoder with residual blocks and skip connections; Project & Excite (PE) blocks inserted after each ResNet module in encoder/decoder; 3D Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) at bottleneck; 2×2×2 max-pooling downsampling and 2×2×2 3D deconvolution upsampling; final 1×1×1 conv with sigmoid for multiclass output.
loss_function: Not specified in provided text
preprocessing: Not specified in provided text (mentions anisotropic z-direction variation in dataset but no explicit resampling/normalization described).
postprocessing: None (explicitly states no post-processing required).
datasets: ['SegTHOR 2019']
dataset_size: SegTHOR: 40 training, 20 testing; used 32 train / 8 validation / 20 test CT scans
comparison_methods: ['Dense V-Net (Feng et al.)', '2D T-net (Kondratenko et al.)', '3D U-Net multi-task (He et al.)', '2D dilated residual U-Net (Vesal et al.)', 'VB-Net / customized V-Net (Han et al.)', 'Cascaded two-step approach (Kim et al.)', 'Two-stage 3D U-Net (Chen et al.)', 'Coarse-to-fine network (Zhang et al.)']
gpu_used: NVIDIA GPU with 12 GB RAM (model not specified)
training_time: 40 minutes
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Not explicitly stated; implied challenges include low soft-tissue contrast, large shape/position variation, anisotropic volumes, limited training data and computational complexity of 3D models.
future_work: Explore additional deep learning techniques to incorporate into the proposed 3D model for improved segmentation of small organs at risk.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel hybrid 3D-ResNet encoder-decoder with 3D ASPP and Project&Excite modules for 3D volumetric multi-organ (OAR) segmentation on CT (SegTHOR). Uses 3D processing, reports quantitative metrics (Dice, HD, VOE, ASSD, RVD) and compares to prior methods.

title: Segmentation of Organs at Risk in Chest Cavity Using 3D Deep Neural Network
venue: IEEE (conference proceedings; exact venue not specified in provided text)
architecture_details: Vanilla 3D U-Net encoder replaced with ResNet-50; added shortcut connections between first activation layers after each down-sampling in encoder/decoder; batch normalization before all activations; transposed convolutions replaced by 3D up-sampling; softmax output; two-stage multi-resolution pipeline (coarse network for VOI center localization + fine network for VOI segmentation); ensemble of 5 CV models at inference.
loss_function: Generalized Dice Loss (class-weighted); RMSprop optimizer
preprocessing: Clip HU to [-1200, 600] and normalize to [0,1]; coarse-resolution data resampled to 6x6x6 mm; masks of esophagus and trachea dilated by 10 mm (original spacing) to prevent label diminishing; training crops to fixed 64x128x128.
postprocessing: Coarse model predicts rough mask -> compute organ center (bounding-box center) -> crop VOI (fixed size 256^3) for fine model; keep only organ-specific positive predictions per VOI; connected component filtering: largest 3D CC for heart/trachea/aorta; for esophagus keep CCs with size > 500 voxels.
datasets: ['SegTHOR (expanded thoracic OAR CT dataset)']
dataset_size: 60 CT scans total (40 train with 5-fold CV; 20 test/evaluation)
comparison_methods: ['Miaofei Han et al. (Multi-resolution VB-nets)', 'Tao He et al. (Multi-task learning)', 'Qin Wang et al. (3D enhanced multi-scale network)', 'Pan Chen et al. (Two-stage network)']
training_time: Not specified (trained 100 epochs; 100 iterations/epoch; batch size 4)
inference_time: Approximately 20-25 seconds per CT image
limitations: Does not outperform top existing approaches on SegTHOR; performance particularly lower for esophagus compared to leading methods.
future_work: Improve model architecture and system pipeline to outperform existing approaches in both segmentation performance and speed.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel 3D U-Net-like deep network with ResNet-50 backbone and multi-resolution VOI localization for multi-organ (OAR) segmentation in thoracic CT; evaluates on SegTHOR CT dataset with Dice and Hausdorff metrics.

title: Automatic Multi-organ Segmentation on Abdominal CT with Dense V-networks
architecture_details: 3D FCN with dense feature stacks (DenseNet-style), V-network down/upsampling with bilinear upsampling to final resolution, dilated convolutions, explicit trainable spatial prior added to logits, and proposed batch-wise spatial dropout; Monte Carlo inference with dropout (mode of 30 samples).
loss_function: Weighted sum of L2 regularization and label-smoothed probabilistic Dice per organ with additional Dice hinge losses (thresholds 0.01 and 0.1 introduced after warm-up iterations); softmax over 9 classes.
preprocessing: Manual cropping to rib-cage/abdominal cavity; resampling cropped ROI to 144^3 volume; data augmentation via affine perturbations yielding skewed subregions 0–10% smaller per dimension.
postprocessing: Resample 72^3 labels back to original cropped region/original resolution; curvature flow smoothing (2 iterations); keep union of connected components comprising >10% of segmented organ volume for each organ.
datasets: ['TCIA Pancreas-CT', 'BTCV (Beyond the Cranial Vault) segmentation challenge']
dataset_size: 90 CT subjects total (43 Pancreas-CT + 47 BTCV); 9-fold cross-validation
comparison_methods: ['VNet', 'VoxResNet', 'DEEDS+JLF (MALF)']
framework: TensorFlow (DenseVNet in NiftyNet); Caffe used for VNet baseline; VoxResNet trained with same protocol (framework not explicitly stated here, but DenseVNet implementation is TensorFlow).
gpu_used: NVIDIA Titan X Pascal or NVIDIA P100
training_time: Approximately 6 hours per network instance (5000 iterations; minibatch size 10; ~625 epochs)
inference_time: Monte Carlo inference (mode of 30 samples) ~8–15 seconds per image
code_url: http://niftynet.io/model_zoo (DenseVNet model in NiftyNet); batch-wise spatial dropout layer in NiftyNet repositories
limitations: Duodenum segmentation least accurate and showed smaller/non-significant improvements; 95% Hausdorff distance exhibited high variability; some organs absent due to prior surgeries (excluded from aggregate stats).
future_work: Not explicitly stated in provided text (discussion notes need to define clinically acceptable accuracies; suggests further work on challenging organs like duodenum and robustness).
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep-learning DenseVNet for multi-organ abdominal CT segmentation using 3D volumetric processing; evaluates on 90 CTs with Dice/mean boundary distance/95% Hausdorff and compares against VNet, VoxResNet, and MALF (DEEDS+JLF).

title: Two-Stage Multi-Organ Automatic Segmentation with Low GPU Memory Occupancy
venue: Proceedings of the 4th WRC Symposium on Advanced Robotics and Automation (WRC SARA)
architecture_details: Tiny-CED Net: lightweight 3D U-Net-inspired encoder-decoder with 4 resolution levels, BatchNorm added, upsampling replaced by transposed convolutions, padding before each transposed conv to match feature map sizes; 1 input channel and 13 output channels; ~1.328M parameters, 33.263G FLOPs. Two-stage pipeline: coarse low-res whole-volume segmentation then per-organ ROI cropping and fine segmentation (one structure using one network).
loss_function: Weighted cross-entropy + Dice loss (summation with trade-off parameter λ)
preprocessing: Coarse stage: resample/standardize spacing to 4.4×2.5×2.5 mm; cap in-plane resolution to max 128×176; crop into 64×128×176 sub-volumes/patches. Fine stage: locate each organ from coarse result, crop ROI, reshape to fit max input size (48×144×192) under <2GB GPU memory constraint.
postprocessing: Two-stage refinement via organ localization and ROI-based fine segmentation (no additional explicit postprocessing such as connected components filtering described).
datasets: ['FLARE22']
dataset_size: 50 labeled abdominal CT volumes (40 train / 10 test); unlabeled FLARE data not used
organs_segmented: ['liver', 'spleen', 'pancreas', 'right kidney', 'left kidney', 'stomach', 'gallbladder', 'esophagus', 'aorta', 'inferior vena cava', 'right adrenal gland', 'left adrenal gland', 'duodenum']
dice_per_organ: {'liver': 0.96, 'right kidney': 0.95, 'spleen': 0.93, 'pancreas': 0.72, 'aorta': 0.92, 'inferior vena cava': 0.9, 'right adrenal gland': 0.73, 'left adrenal gland': 0.7, 'gallbladder': 0.72, 'esophagus': 0.81, 'stomach': 0.88, 'duodenum': 0.65, 'left kidney': 0.94}
other_metrics: {'ASSD_mm_avg': 0.92, 'SSD_mm_avg': 2.33, 'ASSD_mm_per_organ': {'liver': 0.69, 'right kidney': 0.46, 'spleen': 0.31, 'pancreas': 1.6, 'aorta': 0.46, 'inferior vena cava': 0.81, 'right adrenal gland': 0.86, 'left adrenal gland': 0.79, 'gallbladder': 1.72, 'esophagus': 0.84, 'stomach': 1.23, 'duodenum': 1.86, 'left kidney': 0.29}, 'SSD_mm_per_organ': {'liver': 1.82, 'right kidney': 1.14, 'spleen': 2.14, 'pancreas': 3.11, 'aorta': 1.45, 'inferior vena cava': 1.56, 'right adrenal gland': 1.55, 'left adrenal gland': 1.98, 'gallbladder': 3.48, 'esophagus': 2.07, 'stomach': 2.81, 'duodenum': 5.43, 'left kidney': 1.71}}
comparison_methods: ['Standard 3D U-Net (nnU-Net-like training strategy)']
inference_time: <25 s average time consumption (as reported; stage breakdown not specified)
limitations: Lower stability than 3D U-Net (higher surface distance deviation/SSD); coarse stage struggles on small organs (e.g., adrenal glands, duodenum) due to low input resolution; robustness on small organs needs improvement.
future_work: Improve robustness/performance on small organs.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel lightweight 3D CNN (Tiny-CED Net) within a two-stage framework for 13-organ abdominal CT segmentation on FLARE22, uses 3D volumetric processing, reports quantitative metrics (DSC, ASSD, SSD) and compares against 3D U-Net with improved memory usage and slightly better average DSC.

title: Deep learning-based volumetry of the future liver remnants and prediction of candidates for major hepatectomy
architecture_details: DL-FLRVS consists of five separate 3D U-Net models (per Çiçek et al. 3D U-Net) for segmentation of: whole liver, Couinaud segments, hepatic veins, portal veins, and hepatic lesions; multi-stage pipeline for FLR computation and candidate prediction.
loss_function: Not reported in provided text
preprocessing: Not fully reported; uses contrast-enhanced CT with inclusion/exclusion criteria (e.g., slice thickness ≤2 mm; clear portal/hepatic veins). CT acquisition details referenced in Appendix E1.
postprocessing: Not reported in provided text (3D visualization and volumetric calculations performed; specific morphological postprocessing not described).
datasets: ['Internal retrospective hospital CT cohorts (Hospital-1 training; Hospital-2 external validation/comparative cohorts)']
dataset_size: Training: five model-specific training sets n=307 (whole liver), n=170 (Couinaud segments), n=170 (hepatic veins), n=170 (portal veins), n=492 (hepatic lesions). External validation: n=178 total (cohort-1 n=146; cohort-2 n=32). Comparative analysis pool mentioned: 1774 patients at Hospital-2.
comparison_methods: ['hepatic VCAR (semi-automated workstation program)', 'human doctor manual measurements']
training_time: Not reported
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Reference standard was human doctor volumetry rather than post-resection remnant on follow-up CT; functional assessment via Gd-EOB-DTPA MRI not included; used baseline 3D U-Net and did not benchmark against other modern architectures (e.g., nnU-Net, transformer-based).
future_work: Prospective study using actual post-resection remnant for validation; incorporate functional/volumetric MRI (Gd-EOB-DTPA) for more comprehensive assessment; benchmark/optimize with other contemporary segmentation architectures.
screening_notes: Develops a deep learning system (five 3D U-Net models) for automated multi-level liver anatomy segmentation on contrast-enhanced CT, used for FLR volumetry and candidate categorization; uses 3D volumetric processing and reports quantitative evaluation (correlations, Bland-Altman, ROC/AUC, sensitivity/specificity).

title: Dosimetric impact of deep learning-based CT auto-segmentation on radiation therapy treatment planning for prostate cancer
architecture_details: Based on V-Net; 5-level encoder-decoder with residual connections; channels 16/32/64/128/256; 5x5x5 kernels; downsampling via 2x2x2 stride-2 conv; up-convolution in decoder; skip concatenations; GroupNorm after each conv; PReLU activations; softmax output with 0.5 threshold; trained as separate single-label models for prostate, bladder, rectum; input cropped to 128x128x128.
loss_function: Dice loss (DSC-based), applied to structure and background
preprocessing: DICOM RT-struct point clouds converted to binary masks using plastimatch; resampling to 1x1x1 mm (linear interp for images, nearest-neighbor for masks); intensity truncation to soft-tissue window [-150, 150] HU then rescaled to [-1, 1]; cropping central 128x128x128 around prostate center-of-mass.
postprocessing: Softmax + thresholding at 0.5 to obtain binary mask (no additional postprocessing reported).
datasets: ['LMU Munich in-house prostate cancer pelvic CT dataset']
dataset_size: 69 CT scans total (split: 47 train / 11 val / 11 test); excluded 1 hip-implant case and 9 fiducial-marker cases due to artifacts
comparison_methods: ['Manual expert delineations (reference)', 'Literature comparison to Balagopal et al., Sultana et al., Tong et al. (not direct re-implementation)']
framework: Keras (Adam optimizer mentioned as Keras implementation)
training_time: Trained up to ~20k epochs (batch size 2); stopped when validation loss plateaued (exact wall-clock time not reported).
limitations: In-house dataset not publicly available; model fails on CTs with strong metal/fiducial artifacts (reported poor DSC/HD on excluded cases); cropping to 128^3 may underestimate HD and truncates parts of bladder/rectum; multi-physician OAR contours introduce inter-observer variability; human review still required due to occasional low gamma pass rates and one notable failure case.
future_work: Collect larger artifact-containing dataset to train/robustify model; organ-specific cropping/windowing for bladder/rectum; extend to higher-stage prostate cancer by including seminal vesicles (may require more data due to variability).
screening_notes: Uses a deep learning 3D U-Net (V-Net-based) for volumetric CT organ segmentation (prostate, bladder, rectum) with quantitative evaluation (DSC, Hausdorff; also dosimetric metrics). Presents a trained model and a dosimetric impact analysis as the main contribution.

title: Segmentation-based quantitative measurements in renal CT imaging using deep learning
architecture_details: Two separate but identical 3D U-Net models for contrast-enhanced (CECT) and noncontrast (NCCT) CT; residual connections in encoder/decoder blocks; dropout 0.3; patch-based training (96^3) and sliding-window inference with overlap 0.5; 3 classes (background, right kidney, left kidney).
loss_function: Dice loss
preprocessing: NCCT resampled to isotropic 1.5 mm^3 (trilinear); CE kept at original resolution; HU windowing: NC 65/170, CE 200/300; min-max normalization to [0,1]; one-hot labels (3 classes); augmentation: random patch extraction, random flips (x/y/z), random intensity shift (<0.1), Gaussian noise (mean 0, std 0.05).
postprocessing: Argmax to labels; connected component analysis per kidney class keeping largest component; NC masks upsampled back to original resolution (trilinear) with threshold selected on validation set to mitigate interpolation artifacts.
datasets: ['Dataset 1 (kidney donors; CE and NC)', 'Test set 2 (institutional CE from PACS)', 'Test set 3 (PCCT virtual monoenergetic 60 keV and 190 keV)', 'Test set 4 (low-dose NCCT)']
dataset_size: Dataset 1: 88 subjects (each with CE and NC); Test set 2: 18; Test set 3 (PCCT): 15; Test set 4: 8. (Dataset 1 split into train/val/test with ratios 0.65/0.175/0.175; Test set 1 derived from Dataset 1 test split, n=16).

comparison_methods: ['TotalSegmentator (3 mm model for CE; 1.5 mm model for NC)', 'Ellipsoid volume estimation model (baseline for volume)']
training_time: CECT model ~9 h; NCCT model ~4 h; trained for 1500 epochs
inference_time: Per-scan inference time reported (e.g., Test set 1: CE 7.9±1.3 s; NC 4.6±0.6 s; TotalSegmentator baseline: CE 8.9±1.0 s, NC 46.3±6.2 s).
code_url: Available from corresponding author upon reasonable request (no public URL).
limitations: Training data from a single institution with same protocol; relatively small external test sets; training cohort mainly healthy donors so may not generalize to unhealthy kidneys; did not segment cortex/medulla separately; further evaluation needed across broader dose levels/protocols.
future_work: Incorporate general population/unhealthy kidneys to study failure modes and improve adaptation; larger studies across dose levels and protocols; extend to separate cortex and medulla segmentation/quantification.
screening_notes: Proposes and evaluates a deep learning primary method (3D U-Net with residual units) for 3D kidney (left/right) organ segmentation on CT (contrast and noncontrast), reports quantitative metrics (DSC, ICC, volume/axis errors), and includes methodological contribution (dedicated CE/NC models + quantitative axis measurement pipeline; benchmark vs TotalSegmentator).

title: Deep Learning Renal Segmentation for Fully Automated Radiation Dose Estimation in Unsealed Source Therapy
architecture_details: Modified from Pazhitnykh et al.; 21 convolutional layers with 3×3×3 kernels, batch normalization + ReLU; 2×2×2 max pooling downsampling and symmetric upsampling path; skip connections via concatenation (U-Net methodology); 3-class output (left kidney, right kidney, background); input volume 128×128×64.
loss_function: Dice coefficient loss (multi-class: left kidney, right kidney, background)
preprocessing: Rigid registration aligning bony anatomy to a reference patient; automatic cropping to a kidney search volume (~334 mm × 334 mm × 320 mm); resampling/downsampling from native CT resolution (e.g., 0.98×0.98×5.0 mm) to ~2.61×2.61×5.0 mm to fit 128×128×64 input; extensive data augmentation (random noise, edge enhancement, Gaussian smoothing, global HU shift, translation, in-plane rotation).
postprocessing: Upsampling predicted label map back to native CT resolution; smoothing; fusion with original uncropped image; export as DICOM-RT structure set; (noted potential threshold adjustment >0.5 to correct systematic over-segmentation; suggested removal of small non-contiguous labels as possible post-processing).
datasets: ['Institutional SPECT/CT low-dose CT cohort for training (post-treatment 177Lu-octreotate and other clinical CTs)', '177Lu-PSMA-617 Phase II trial SPECT/CT cohort for testing (ANZCTR12615000912583)']
dataset_size: Training: 89 manually contoured patients (augmented 7× to 712 samples; 640 used for training, 72 for validation). Testing: 24 patients (right and left kidneys).
other_metrics: {'mean_distance_to_agreement_mm': {'right_kidney_mean': 2.0, 'right_kidney_sd': 1.0, 'left_kidney_mean': 4.0, 'left_kidney_sd': 7.5, 'left_kidney_mean_excluding_outlier': 2.5, 'left_kidney_sd_excluding_outlier': 1.7}, 'inference_time_seconds': 'CNN inference 10–15 s; end-to-end pipeline ~90 s (including registration and post-processing).'}
comparison_methods: ['Expert manual kidney contours (ground truth comparison)']
framework: Keras (v2.0.8) with TensorFlow backend (v1.3) in Python
training_time: Approximately 2.5 days (300 epochs)
inference_time: CNN contour detection 10–15 s; total processing typically <90 s
limitations: Reduced performance on cystic kidneys due to under-representation in training data; occasional inclusion of small detached bowel regions; one poor case associated with CT streak artifact; systematic ~7% larger volumes likely due to upsampling/smoothing; authors advise contour review before clinical use.
future_work: Retrain with additional challenging cases (e.g., polycystic kidneys) to improve robustness; extend framework to a more comprehensive set of organs for nuclear medicine dosimetry; for smaller organs/tumors use tighter search volume or sliding window at near-native resolution; potentially use fused SPECT/CT or PET/CT as CNN input to leverage complementary features.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel 3D CNN (U-Net-like) for kidney (organ) segmentation on non-contrast CT volumes, uses 3D context (3D kernels), reports quantitative evaluation (Dice, mean distance-to-agreement, volume) and compares to expert manual contours; includes methodological contributions (3D CNN + preprocessing/postprocessing workflow, Dice loss, augmentation, PACS integration).

title: Scribble-based 3D Multiple Abdominal Organ Segmentation via Triple-branch Multi-dilated Network with Pixel- and Class-wise Consistency
architecture_details: TDNet: shared encoder with three decoders using different dilation rates (1, 3, 6); different decoder initializations (Kaiming/Xavier/Normal) and bottleneck feature dropout before auxiliary decoders; ensemble-averaged soft pseudo labels; final inference uses primary decoder; sliding-window inference; no post-processing.
loss_function: Partially supervised cross-entropy on scribble voxels (pCE) + Uncertainty-weighted Soft Pseudo label Consistency (USPC) using voxel-wise entropy weights and KL divergence + Multi-view Projection-based Class-similarity Consistency (MPCC) using KL divergence on class affinity matrices; ramp-up weighting for unsupervised losses (alpha, beta).
preprocessing: HU window/level 400/50; intensity normalization to [0,1]; patch-based training (patch size 80×96×96).
postprocessing: None (explicitly states no post-processing).
datasets: ['WORD']
dataset_size: 150 CT volumes total (100 train / 20 val / 30 test); each volume 159–330 slices of 512×512; scribble annotations provided for training volumes in axial view.
comparison_methods: ['FullySup (3D U-Net)', 'pCE', 'TV (Total Variation Loss)', 'USTM', 'EM (Entropy Minimization)', 'DMPLS']
limitations: Relies on scribble annotations (weak supervision) and is evaluated on a single public dataset (WORD); performance for very sparse labels not demonstrated in this paper.
future_work: Explore the effect of the method on sparser labels, e.g., volumetric data with scribble annotations on one or few slices.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep-learning 3D framework (TDNet) for multi-organ abdominal CT segmentation using 3D U-Net backbone with triple decoders and new consistency losses; operates on 3D CT volumes (WORD), segments 7 organs, and reports quantitative metrics (DSC, ASD, HD95) with comparisons and ablations.

title: Rethinking Abdominal Organ Segmentation (RAOS) in the clinical scenario: A robustness evaluation benchmark with challenging cases
architecture: Benchmark study (multiple architectures: nnU-Net, 3D UX-Net, nnFormer, Swin UNETR, UNETR, TransBTS, REPUXNET)
architecture_details: Introduces RAOS dataset (413 CTs, 17/19 organs) and robustness benchmark across three clinical subsets (no surgery; surgery without organ missing; surgery with organ missing). Evaluates 7 SOTA 3D DL segmentation methods trained on SetA and tested on SetB/SetC; introduces organ hallucination ratio metric for missing-organ cases; reports cross-dataset generalization (BTCV, AMOS, AbdomenCT-1K).
loss_function: Not specified (uses default settings of each baseline implementation, incl. nnU-Net defaults).
preprocessing: Not specified (uses each baseline's default preprocessing; nnU-Net self-configuring pipeline implied).
postprocessing: Not specified (uses each baseline's default post-processing if any).
datasets: ['RAOS', 'BTCV', 'AMOS', 'AbdomenCT-1K']
dataset_size: RAOS: 413 CT scans from 413 patients; subsets: SetA 317 (with 220 train / 67 internal eval mentioned), SetB 22, SetC 74
organs_segmented: ['liver', 'spleen', 'left kidney', 'right kidney', 'stomach', 'gallbladder', 'esophagus', 'pancreas', 'duodenum', 'colon', 'intestine', 'left adrenal', 'right adrenal', 'rectum', 'bladder', 'left head of femur', 'right head of femur', 'prostate', 'seminal vesicles']
dice_per_organ: {'SetA_nnUNet': {'liver': 0.9665, 'spleen': 0.9593, 'left kidney': 0.958, 'right kidney': 0.9556, 'stomach': 0.9277, 'gallbladder': 0.8063, 'esophagus': 0.8323, 'pancreas': 0.8328, 'duodenum': 0.7205, 'colon': 0.871, 'intestine': 0.8794, 'right adrenal': 0.7182, 'left adrenal': 0.7268, 'rectum': 0.831, 'bladder': 0.9486, 'left head of femur': 0.8499, 'right head of femur': 0.8679, 'prostate': 0.9301, 'seminal vesicles': 0.8823}}
