training_time: Not reported
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Benchmark shows substantial performance drop on clinically challenging post-surgery cases (especially organ-missing), with frequent organ hallucination; current SOTA methods not clinically applicable for these corner cases.
future_work: Extend RAOS with more clinically challenging cases to support development of clinically acceptable robust segmentation methods.
screening_notes: CT-based abdominal multi-organ segmentation benchmark using deep learning baselines (3D nnU-Net, UNETR, SwinUNETR, etc.) with quantitative evaluation (DSC, NSD) and a novel robustness/hallucination analysis plus a new dataset (RAOS) and metric.

title: Hierarchical 3D fully convolutional networks for multi-organ segmentation
architecture_details: Two-stage hierarchical coarse-to-fine approach: Stage 1 trained on body-mask candidate region C1 (~40% voxels) to get coarse multi-organ predictions; Stage 2 fine-tuned/trained on dilated foreground candidate region C2 (~10% voxels) for refined segmentation. Uses 3D U-Net with 4 resolution levels, skip connections, 3x3x3 convs, 2x2x2 pooling/up-conv, final 1x1x1 conv to 8 classes; batch normalization; tiling/sliding-window inference with optional overlapping tiles and probability averaging.
loss_function: Weighted voxel-wise softmax cross-entropy (class weights based on inverse frequency within candidate region); argmax for final labels
preprocessing: Downsample CT by factor 2 for training/inference field-of-view; Stage 1 candidate region via thresholding + morphological operations (hole filling, largest component) to create body mask; random sub-volume sampling (132x132x116) from candidate regions; data augmentation with 3D elastic (B-spline) deformations and small rotations (-5 to +5 degrees).
postprocessing: No post-processing on final FCN outputs; for generating Stage 2 candidate region: dilate any foreground label in 3D with radius r (chosen r=3) to form C2; nearest-neighbor resampling back to original resolution; overlapping-tile probability averaging during inference (optional).
datasets: ['In-house clinical contrast-enhanced abdominal CT dataset (portal venous phase) for training/validation (gastric surgery planning)', 'External unseen abdominal CT dataset from different hospital (gastric cancer patients) with liver/spleen/pancreas labels']
dataset_size: Training/validation: 331 CT (281 train, 50 val). External test: 150 CT.
other_metrics: {'sensitivity_recall_stage1_candidate_generation': '>99% average at dilation radius r=3 (reported as sensitivity/recall)', 'false_positive_rate_stage1_candidate_generation': '~10% average at r=3 (reported as FPR)'}
comparison_methods: ['Tong et al. (2015) (global affine + ML)', 'Wolz et al. (2013) (local non-rigid registration/atlas)', 'Oda et al. (2016) (regression forest + graph cut)', 'Roth et al. (2017) (2D FCN)', 'Zhou et al. (2016) (2D FCN coarse-to-fine)']
gpu_used: NVIDIA GeForce GTX TITAN X (12GB) for training; NVIDIA GeForce 1080 (8GB) for testing
training_time: 2-3 days for 200k iterations (stage 1) on 281 cases (stage 2 additional 115k iterations; time not separately stated)
inference_time: Per volume: ~1.4-3.3 minutes per stage (non-overlapping); ~1.6-4.4 minutes per stage with overlapping tiles in stage 2 (depending on candidate region size)
limitations: GPU memory constraints require fixed-size sub-volumes and tiling; class imbalance motivates weighted loss and hierarchical candidate regions; direct comparison to other methods is difficult due to differing datasets/evaluation schemes; performance varies by organ (smaller/thinner organs more challenging).
future_work: Suggests the hierarchical approach should generalize to other 3D CNN/FCN architectures and potentially other 3D image modalities.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel hierarchical coarse-to-fine two-stage 3D FCN (3D U-Net) for multi-organ abdominal CT segmentation, processes 3D volumes with 3D convolutions, and reports quantitative Dice results on validation and an external test set.

title: Deep learning to achieve clinically applicable segmentation of head and neck anatomy for radiotherapy
architecture_details: 3D U-Net for delineating 21 head-and-neck organs-at-risk (OARs); paper emphasizes clinically applicable evaluation and introduces surface Dice similarity coefficient (surface DSC) with organ-specific tolerances. (Further architectural hyperparameters not provided in the supplied text.)
loss_function: Not specified in the provided text
preprocessing: CT planning scans with standard spacing ~0.976×0.976×2.5mm; non-standard spacing excluded except 1.25mm scans which were subsampled; TCIA non-CT planning scans and those not meeting 2.5mm slice thickness excluded; PDDCA used with varying voxel sizes (2–3mm slice thickness, 0.98–1.27mm in-plane).
postprocessing: Not specified in the provided text
datasets: ['UCLH (internal clinical planning CT dataset)', 'TCIA (TCGA-HNSC and Head-Neck Cetuximab subsets; re-labeled by authors)', 'PDDCA (MICCAI 2015 Head & Neck OAR challenge dataset)']
dataset_size: UCLH: 663 train scans (389 patients), 100 val scans (51 patients), 75 test scans (46 patients) with 21 adjudicated test scans (19 patients) used for primary expert comparison; TCIA: 7 val scans (6 patients), 24 test scans (24 patients) (text also mentions 28 scans in some tables/figures); PDDCA: 15 test scans (15 patients).
other_metrics: {'surface_DSC': 'Surface Dice similarity coefficient (surface DSC) at organ-specific tolerance τ (mm), introduced by the paper; reported on UCLH and TCIA test sets (boxplots/figures).', 'volumetric_DSC': 'Standard volumetric Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) reported for comparison to prior work (Table 1).'}
comparison_methods: ['Human radiographers (independent segmentations) vs oncologist-adjudicated ground truth', 'Prior published deep-learning OAR segmentation works (indirect comparison via reported volumetric DSC in Table 1)']
framework: Not specified in the provided text
gpu_used: Not specified in the provided text
training_time: Not specified in the provided text
inference_time: Not specified in the provided text
limitations: Key limitations noted include: only CT planning scans (no MRI/PET; some OARs like optic chiasm excluded due to needing MRI); some OAR omissions (e.g., oral cavity) due to insufficient protocol-conformant training examples; limited number of oncologists for ground truth may not capture full variability and may bias tolerances; ambiguity in some structures (e.g., brainstem boundaries); protected-characteristic status not available so fairness analysis not possible; performance lower on some OARs (e.g., right lens, brainstem) on external TCIA data possibly due to image quality and labeling variability.
future_work: Clinical studies to define acceptability and measure real-world time savings; incorporate MRI to segment additional structures; address missing OAR classes and lymph node segmentation; improve evaluation across demographics and assess intersectional fairness; further validation and regulatory approvals for clinical deployment.
screening_notes: Primary method is deep learning (3D U-Net) for multi-organ (21 OARs) segmentation on CT planning scans; uses 3D volumetric context; reports quantitative metrics (surface DSC and volumetric DSC) and introduces a novel metric (surface DSC) plus clinically-focused evaluation and dataset release.

title: A Method of Rapid Quantification of Patient-Specific Organ Dose for CT Using Coupled Deep-Learning based Multi-Organ Segmentation and GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo Dose Computing
architecture_details: 3D U-Net–based encoder-decoder with 4 repeated residual blocks in encoder (each block: 4 conv modules with 3x3x3 kernels, instance norm, leaky ReLU; last conv uses stride 2x2x2 for downsampling; spatial dropout between early conv modules). Decoder has 4 segmentation blocks (2 conv modules + 1 deconv module) with skip connections via concatenation. Deep supervision-like multi-scale outputs: in final 3 segmentation blocks, 1x1x1 conv maps to class probabilities and results are merged by upsampling; final SoftMax. Additional 'fine segmentation' stage for small/elongated organs (esophagus, spinal cord) using cropped volumes and separate networks.
loss_function: Weighted Dice similarity coefficient loss (negative Dice; epsilon=1)
preprocessing: HU clipping to [-200, 300] then normalized to [0,1]; body-contour ROI cropping to suppress background; downsampling/resampling (linear interpolation for images, nearest for labels). LCTSC resampled to 2x2x2.5 mm; fine segmentation for esophagus/spinal cord resampled to 1x1x2.5 mm. PCT resampled to 144x144x144 voxels. Patch extraction for training (LCTSC 96^3, PCT 128^3; fine segmentation 32^3).
postprocessing: Sliding-window inference with overlapping patches; merge patch probability tensors by mean in overlap; argmax to assign class per voxel; resample segmentation back to original CT size via nearest-neighbor interpolation. Fine segmentation results for esophagus/spinal cord filled back into initial multi-organ segmentation.
datasets: ['Lung CT Segmentation Challenge 2017 (LCTSC)', 'Pancreas-CT (PCT)']
dataset_size: LCTSC: 60 thoracic CT patients; PCT: 43 abdominal contrast-enhanced CT patients (total 103)
dice_per_organ: {'LCTSC_median': {'right lung': 0.97, 'left lung': 0.96, 'heart': 0.93, 'spinal cord': 0.88, 'esophagus': 0.78}, 'PCT_median': {'spleen': 0.96, 'liver': 0.96, 'left kidney': 0.95, 'stomach': 0.89, 'gall bladder': 0.87, 'pancreas': 0.79, 'esophagus': 0.74, 'duodenum': 0.64}, 'LCTSC_mean_std_our_method': {'left lung': '0.96±0.01', 'right lung': '0.96±0.02', 'heart': '0.93±0.02', 'esophagus': '0.75±0.10', 'spinal cord': '0.88±0.04'}}
comparison_methods: ['LCTSC challenge reference and 7 contestant methods (DSC comparison)', 'Population-average phantom based organ dose estimation (VirtualDose workflow) vs patient-specific segmentation-based dose']
gpu_used: Nvidia GeForce Titan X (12GB) for segmentation; Nvidia Titan RTX (24GB) reported for ARCHER dose computing speed
inference_time: Segmentation <5 s per patient; Monte Carlo dose computing (1e8 photons) <4 s per patient (<0.5% statistical uncertainty)
limitations: Lower/variable accuracy for narrow/long or low-contrast structures (esophagus, duodenum), likely due to limited training data and variability; did not assess impact of major abnormalities; diffuse abnormalities and low intra-abdominal fat may degrade segmentation; limited organ coverage (e.g., head/neck) and population coverage (e.g., pediatrics).
future_work: Use larger datasets; include children and additional radiosensitive organs (e.g., head/neck); evaluate robustness to abnormalities; test on annotated cadaver CT datasets from MGH.
screening_notes: Primary method is a deep-learning 3D U-Net–based CNN for multi-organ segmentation on CT volumes, evaluated with Dice (and dose error metrics). Uses 3D patches/volumes (3D convs) and reports quantitative results on two CT datasets with a described architecture and training/testing pipeline.

title: Automatic Liver Segmentation Using an Adversarial Image-to-Image Network
architecture_details: DI2IN: 3D convolutional encoder-decoder with skip/feature concatenation (bridges), multi-branch deep supervision, bilinear upscaling in decoder (no deconvolution), 3x3x3 kernels, Leaky ReLU + batch norm. DI2IN-AN adds a CNN discriminator used only during training to provide adversarial loss.
loss_function: Weighted sum of voxel-wise binary cross-entropy losses for deep supervision branches + final output; adversarial loss term added for generator (GAN objective with log D(G(x)) variant); discriminator trained with binary cross-entropy.
preprocessing: Down-sampled CT volumes to 3.0 mm isotropic resolution.
postprocessing: None (CRF/graph-cut refinement tried but worsened results; not used).
datasets: ['Private annotated CT dataset (1000+ volumes) for training', 'Private independent test set (50 volumes)', 'MICCAI-SLiver07 (mentioned as public dataset; not used for main reported results)']
dataset_size: 1000+ CT volumes for training; 50 additional CT volumes for independent testing (all expert-annotated).
comparison_methods: ['Ling et al. hierarchical learning-based method (hand-crafted features) [5]', 'DI2IN (no adversarial training) trained on 400 cases', 'DI2IN-AN trained on 400 cases', 'DI2IN trained on 1000+ cases']
training_time: DI2IN training: 200 iterations (SGD, batch size 4); adversarial training: 100 overall iterations (kD=10, batch size 8; kG=1, batch size 4).
inference_time: < 1 second per case (discriminator not used at inference).
limitations: Not explicitly stated; notes that CRF/graph-cut post-processing can cause leakage and worsen results on low-contrast/fuzzy boundaries; method relies on downsampling to 3.0mm for efficiency.
future_work: Not explicitly stated.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep learning method (DI2IN with adversarial training) for 3D liver organ segmentation from 3D CT volumes, using 3D convolutions and reporting quantitative metrics (Dice, ASD) with comparisons.

title: Towards Automatic Abdominal Multi-Organ Segmentation in Dual Energy CT Using Cascaded 3D Fully Convolutional Network
architecture_details: Two-stage coarse-to-fine cascaded network; each stage is a standard 3D U-Net. Inputs are multi-channel: DECT mixed image (linear alpha blending of low/high kV), a binary body mask from skin-contour thresholding, and labels during training. Stage-1 predicts ROI mask used to crop/mask for stage-2 final segmentation. Fine-tunes a general SECT-trained model (from Roth et al.).
loss_function: Softmax with weighted voxel-wise cross-entropy loss
preprocessing: Linear weighting (alpha blending) of low- and high-energy DECT to form mixed image; generate binary mask by thresholding skin contour; use mixed image + binary mask as multi-channel input; ROI generation via stage-1 for stage-2
postprocessing: Not explicitly described (ROI masking via stage-1 output used as input mask for stage-2)
datasets: ['Clinical torso DECT dataset (University Hospital Erlangen; Siemens SOMATOM Force dual-source CT)']
dataset_size: 42 torso DECT volumes (adult male/female); 8-fold cross-validation; per split ~30 train / 6 val / 6 test
comparison_methods: ['SECT results from Roth et al. (fine-tuned 3D FCN / 3D U-Net) (not directly comparable due to different dataset)']
limitations: Lower accuracy for liver and spleen compared to reported SECT results; performance variability in one fold possibly due to heterogeneous patient diseases and inconsistent symptoms between training/testing; alpha mixing weight is user-defined and may not be optimal per organ; limited to 4 organs and 42 scans.
future_work: Use alpha as data augmentation; modify network to take two DECT images (low/high energy) as separate inputs; include more organs and more scans from different patients.
screening_notes: Proposes a cascaded 3D FCN (two-stage 3D U-Net) for abdominal multi-organ segmentation on dual-energy CT, evaluated on 42 DECT volumes with Dice metrics; includes methodological contribution via DECT preprocessing (alpha-mixed images) and fine-tuning strategy.

title: On the influence of Dice loss function in multi-class organ segmentation of abdominal CT using 3D fully convolutional networks
architecture_details: 3D U-Net-type FCN (similar to Çiçek et al. 3D U-Net) trained on randomly cropped 64×64×64 subvolumes; full-volume inference via subvolume prediction; 8 output maps (7 organs + background).
loss_function: Multi-class Dice loss (continuous Dice) with class-frequency-based weighting: uniform (Wu), simple inverse frequency (Ws), and square inverse frequency (Wq).
preprocessing: Downsampled CT volumes by factor of 4; random cropping of 64×64×64 subvolumes for training.
postprocessing: Not reported.
datasets: ['Private clinical abdominal CT dataset (portal venous phase; pre-operative gastric surgery planning)']
dataset_size: 377 CT volumes total; 340 train / 37 test (random split)
comparison_methods: ['Dice-loss weighting variants: uniform vs simple vs square; learning rate comparison (0.001 vs 0.01)']
training_time: About 2 days for 10,000 iterations (per model) on GTX 1080 8GB
inference_time: Not reported.
limitations: No data augmentation used; performance sensitive to interaction between class-balancing weights and initial learning rate; suggests some models may not have fully converged by 10,000 iterations.
future_work: Study effects of data augmentation; tune weighting/learning-rate per organ and potentially combine models; leverage higher GPU memory and larger datasets for improved performance.
screening_notes: Uses deep learning (3D U-Net/3D FCN) for multi-organ segmentation on 3D abdominal CT volumes, reports quantitative Dice scores, and contributes an experimental study on Dice-loss weighting schemes and learning-rate interactions.

title: Organ at Risk Segmentation in Head and Neck CT Images Using a Two-Stage Segmentation Framework Based on 3D U-Net
venue: IEEE Access (as indicated by template/formatting; exact bibliographic details not fully shown in provided text)
architecture_details: Two cascaded 3D U-Nets: LocNet for bounding-box localization on downsampled volume (96×96×56) and SegNet for organ segmentation within localized target volume; per-organ training (one LocNet + one SegNet per OAR, 18 networks total). Each U-Net uses 3×3×3 conv + BN + ReLU, 2×2×2 maxpool, 2×2×2 up-conv, skip connections; 17 conv layers; final 1×1×1 conv for binary output.
loss_function: Cross-entropy loss (for both LocNet and SegNet), optimized with Adam
preprocessing: Resample CTs to isotropic 1×1×1 mm using bicubic interpolation; automatic cropping to 384×384×224 with organ-group-specific margins; downsample cropped volume by factor 4 to 96×96×56 for LocNet; determine fixed bounding-box sizes per organ (multiples of 8).
postprocessing: Bounding box from LocNet via sliding-window maximizing positive voxels; remove small isolated regions in SegNet output: delete components with volume <10% of total predicted segmentation.
datasets: ['PDDCA / MICCAI 2015 Head and Neck Auto-Segmentation Challenge dataset (v1.4.1)']
dataset_size: 33 training CTs (25 training + 8 additional training), 15 test CTs
organs_segmented: ['Mandible', 'Parotid left', 'Parotid right', 'Brainstem', 'Submandibular left', 'Submandibular right', 'Optic nerve left', 'Optic nerve right', 'Chiasm']
dice_per_organ: {'Mandible': 0.93, 'Parotid left': 0.864, 'Parotid right': 0.848, 'Brainstem': 0.875, 'Submandibular left': 0.758, 'Submandibular right': 0.733, 'Optic nerve left': 0.737, 'Optic nerve right': 0.736, 'Chiasm': 0.451}
other_metrics: {'95HD_mm_per_organ': {'Mandible': 1.26, 'Parotid left': 2.41, 'Parotid right': 2.93, 'Brainstem': 2.01, 'Submandibular left': 2.86, 'Submandibular right': 3.44, 'Optic nerve left': 2.53, 'Optic nerve right': 2.13, 'Chiasm': 2.83}, 'PPV_percent_per_organ': {'Mandible': 94.6, 'Parotid left': 88.3, 'Parotid right': 86.0, 'Brainstem': 88.7, 'Submandibular left': 82.2, 'Submandibular right': 82.5, 'Optic nerve left': 72.5, 'Optic nerve right': 70.6, 'Chiasm': 46.7}, 'SEN_percent_per_organ': {'Mandible': 91.5, 'Parotid left': 84.8, 'Parotid right': 84.2, 'Brainstem': 86.8, 'Submandibular left': 74.4, 'Submandibular right': 69.4, 'Optic nerve left': 75.9, 'Optic nerve right': 78.4, 'Chiasm': 49.8}}
comparison_methods: ['VU [34]', 'UC [32]', 'IM [33]', 'UB [11]', 'Tong et al. [23]', 'Baselines: joint localization, downsampling-only, sliding-window']
training_time: Approximately 30 hours total to train 18 networks (one LocNet + one SegNet for each of 9 OARs); training terminated at 200 iterations over training images; batch size 1
inference_time: ≈6 s per OAR per image (≈2 s network forward + ≈4 s LocNet postprocessing); ≈108 s to segment all 9 OARs for one subject
limitations: Limited training data (33 training images) leading to potential overfitting; low soft-tissue contrast in CT makes boundaries unclear; errors in localization (LocNet) propagate to SegNet; memory limitations required downsampling for mandible target volume depth.
future_work: Suggests higher-resolution interpolation (beyond 1 mm isotropic) may improve accuracy, especially for small OARs; implies improving localization and handling low-contrast boundaries.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel two-stage 3D U-Net framework (LocNet+SegNet) for multi-organ (OAR) segmentation on 3D head-and-neck CT volumes, evaluated on MICCAI 2015 Challenge (PDDCA) with quantitative metrics (DSC, 95HD, PPV, SEN) and comparisons to prior methods.

title: Computation of Total Kidney Volume from CT images in Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease using Multi-Task 3D Convolutional Neural Networks
architecture_details: Multi-task 3D FCN with shared encoder and task-specific decoders (kidney/ADPK and liver). Encoder/decoder with conv blocks (two 3x3x3 conv + BN + ReLU) and 2x2x2 max-pooling; decoder uses stride-2 upconvolutions; long skip connections; 1x1x1 conv to class logits. Network depth increased vs standard 3D U-Net to match large receptive field needed for enlarged ADPK kidneys.
loss_function: Dice loss (multi-class) and bootstrapped cross-entropy loss (top K=10% hardest voxels); multi-task loss is mean of task losses
preprocessing: Resample to 1.5 mm isotropic voxel spacing; crop along z to ~144x250x250 due to memory limits; data augmentation with rotation and scaling
postprocessing: Not reported
datasets: ['ADPK dataset (203 abdominal CTs with kidney masks; from Muto et al. 2017 study)', 'Liver dataset (176 abdominal CTs with liver masks; contrast and non-contrast; many with tumors)']
dataset_size: ADPK: 203 CT volumes (3-fold cross-validation; ~1/4 used for validation each fold). Liver: 176 CT volumes (training only).
comparison_methods: ['2D FCN baseline (implemented per Sharma et al. 2017)', 'Single-task 3D FCN (3D U-Net-like) trained only on ADPK dataset', 'Multi-task 3D FCN with Dice loss', 'Multi-task 3D FCN with bootstrapped cross-entropy']
gpu_used: Reedbush-L (SGI Rackable C2112-4GP3/C1102-GP8) HPC system (GPU model not specified)
training_time: Not reported (states ~100 epochs for all experiments)
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Some cases (e.g., unique test cases or small kidneys) still have >5% absolute TKV error; limited ADPK dataset size; challenges distinguishing renal vs hepatic cysts in contact; dice loss instability when crops contain no kidney voxels is noted.
future_work: Add additional auxiliary tasks (e.g., spleen, colon, or unrelated anatomies like heart/lung) to further improve performance; evaluate bootstrapped cross-entropy on more severe imbalance tasks (e.g., aorta segmentation).
screening_notes: Proposes a novel multi-task 3D FCN (3D U-Net-like) for kidney (ADPKD) segmentation from 3D CT volumes, uses 3D context, reports quantitative Dice and TKV error, and compares against 2D/3D baselines with methodological contributions (multi-task shared encoder + bootstrapped cross-entropy loss).

title: CT organ segmentation using GPU data augmentation, unsupervised labels and IOU loss
architecture_details: 3D GoogLeNet-like FCN with 3D convolutions/pooling and multiple 3D Inception modules; decimation by factor 8 then interpolation; no skip connections; input 120×120×160 at 3mm^3; outputs 6-class voxelwise softmax (5 organs + other).
loss_function: IOU (Jaccard) loss (multi-class average over classes); compared against weighted cross-entropy; discusses relation to Dice loss and family of IOU losses.
preprocessing: Resample CT volumes to 3mm^3 using Gaussian smoothing (anti-aliasing) then interpolation; intensity window/level normalization to [0,1] with random windowing during augmentation; training uses random 120×120×160 sub-volume crops; inference uses overlapping sub-volumes with averaging; final prediction resampled back to original resolution via nearest-neighbor.
postprocessing: Inference uses overlapping sub-volume tiling with averaging in overlap; argmax to get label map; nearest-neighbor upsampling to original resolution. (No additional morphological postprocessing for network outputs described.)
datasets: ["LiTS (Liver Tumor Segmentation Challenge) derived multi-organ dataset (authors' annotations + generated labels)", 'LiTS challenge test set (external evaluation for liver)']
dataset_size: 130 CT scans (multi-organ labels); plus 10 CT scans with manual lung and bone labels for evaluation
comparison_methods: ['Same 3D FCN trained with weighted cross-entropy (with/without augmentation)', 'Morphological label generators for lung and bone (as baselines/label sources)', 'LiTS leaderboard context (qualitative comparison to more complex methods; not direct re-implementation)']
gpu_used: NVIDIA GeForce Titan X Pascal (4 GPUs) (for augmentation speed experiments); CPU Intel Core i7-6900K
inference_time: Average processing time reported for LiTS liver test: 5.98 s per volume; GPU memory ~2429 MB
limitations: Network did not match accuracy of simple morphology for very distinctive organs (lungs, bones) on their evaluation; coarse 3mm^3 resolution and limited field-of-view requires sub-volume tiling; morphology methods can fail catastrophically in unexpected cases; overall performance on LiTS liver is not top-tier compared to more complex pipelines.
future_work: Release trained model/code/dataset publicly; suggests their improvements (augmentation + IOU loss) could be incorporated into more complex/top-performing models; implies potential combination of unsupervised methods as post-processing with neural network outputs.
screening_notes: Primary method is a 3D fully-convolutional deep network for multi-organ CT segmentation; processes 3D sub-volumes with 3D ops, reports quantitative metrics (Dice, VOE, ASSD, etc.), and includes methodological contributions (GPU 3D augmentation pipeline, IOU loss analysis, weak/unsupervised label generation, new 5-organ dataset).

title: 3D Kidneys and Kidney Tumor Semantic Segmentation using Boundary-Aware Networks
architecture: Encoder-decoder CNN (U-Net-like) with residual blocks and boundary-aware branch
architecture_details: Asymmetric 3D encoder-decoder with residual blocks; bilinear upsampling with skip/add connections; additional attention-driven boundary stream (attention gates) supervised with edge-aware losses; final 1x1x1 conv with sigmoid; uses TTA and 5-model ensemble at inference.
loss_function: Dice loss (main + boundary stream) + weighted BCE loss for boundary stream; total loss averaged over tumor-only and foreground (kidney+tumor) predictions
preprocessing: Intensity normalization by dividing by 1000 and clipping to [-1,1]; resample to 1x1x1 mm isotropic for training/inference; training on 176x176x176 crops with biased sampling (0.8 centered on tumor, 0.1 any foreground, 0.1 background)
postprocessing: Resample predictions back to original resolution; test-time augmentation (TTA); ensemble of 5 models
datasets: ['KiTS 2019 (Kidney Tumor Segmentation Challenge)']
dataset_size: 300 patients total; 210 train / 90 test (challenge split); authors also used an internal train/val split from the 210 training cases
screening_notes: Proposes a novel boundary-aware 3D encoder-decoder CNN for kidney and kidney tumor segmentation from 3D CT volumes (KiTS19), uses 3D convolutions and reports quantitative Dice metrics; includes organ (kidney) segmentation with methodological contribution (boundary stream + edge-aware losses).

title: Z-Net: an Anisotropic 3D DCNN for Medical CT Volume Segmentation
architecture_details: Framework replacing isotropic 3D convs with anisotropic spatial separable convolutions: 2D convs in XY (e.g., 3x3x1) plus 1D conv along Z (1x1xD). Two integration modes: (1) replace all 3D convs with 2D+1D (ZU-Net v1, ZV-Net v1); (2) use 2D convs and add a single 1D conv before each down/up-sampling block (ZU-Net v2, ZV-Net v2). Uses Instance Normalization; Patch-512 cropping (512x512x8) with stride 1 in Z for training.
loss_function: Softmax cross-entropy (binary formulation shown); evaluation metric is foreground IoU (Jaccard).
preprocessing: Intensity normalization to [0,1] by dividing by per-volume maximum intensity; patch generation: Patch-512 (512x512x8) stride 1 along Z for training and stride 8 for eval/test; baselines use Patch-128 (128x128x64) with strides 128,128,8 for training; data augmentation via rotations about Z axis (aorta/liver: 90/180/270 degrees; lung: 180 degrees).
postprocessing: Not reported.
datasets: ['VISCERAL', 'SLiver07', 'Lung CT Segmentation Challenge 2017 (AAPM 2017 thoracic autosegmentation challenge)']
dataset_size: Aorta: 20 CT volumes (2-fold CV); Liver: 20 CT volumes (2-fold CV); Lung: 60 CT volumes (36 train / 24 test).
other_metrics: {'IoU': {'best_reported': 0.877, 'details': 'Best mean IoU reported in Table I/II is 0.877 (lung, ZV-Net v1). Aorta best 0.674; Liver best 0.852.'}}
comparison_methods: ['3D U-Net (vanilla)', 'V-Net (vanilla)', '3D U-Net (3 down-sampling layers, Patch-512 ablation)', 'V-Net (3 down-sampling layers, Patch-512 ablation)']
training_time: Reported as seconds per 100 iterations (Table II): 3D U-Net 70.36s; ZU-Net v1 67.97s; ZU-Net v2 67.02s; V-Net (3 down-sampling) 157.4s; ZV-Net v1 82.08s; ZV-Net v2 80.11s.
limitations: Not explicitly stated as limitations; implied constraints include using sub-volumes with limited Z-depth (8 slices) due to GPU memory and evaluation primarily via IoU on three datasets.
future_work: Explore deeper network designs and better kernel decomposition methods to further improve medical volume segmentation performance.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep-learning 3D CT volume segmentation framework (Z-Net) using anisotropic spatial separable convolutions integrated into 3D U-Net/V-Net; operates on 3D sub-volumes (512x512x8) with 3D context; segments organs/anatomy (aorta, liver, lung); CT is primary modality; reports quantitative IoU results and comparisons to baselines.

title: Basic Ensembles of Vanilla-Style Deep Learning Models Improve Liver Segmentation From CT Images
architecture_details: Ensemble of four off-the-shelf/vanilla models: 3D U-Net (NiftyNet), DeepMedic (original implementation), V-Net (NiftyNet), Dense V-Networks (NiftyNet). Non-trainable combiners: majority vote, average, product, min/max. Probability maps thresholded at 0.5; product combiner uses prior foreground probability; smoothing of probability maps with 3x3x3 kernel (MATLAB smooth3).
loss_function: U-Net: cross-entropy (NiftyNet). V-Net: V-Net loss (as per NiftyNet V-Net; not explicitly detailed in text). Dense V-Networks: probabilistic Dice loss. DeepMedic: not specified in provided text.
preprocessing: Not clearly specified beyond using CT volumes; prior foreground probability estimated from training data for product combiner.
postprocessing: Smoothing of each model output probability map and ensemble output using 3x3x3 convolution kernel (MATLAB smooth3); thresholding at 0.5 to obtain binary liver mask.
datasets: ['CHAOS (CT task)', '3D-IRCADb-01 (3Dircadb1)']
dataset_size: CHAOS CT: 40 patients (20 train, 20 test). 3D-IRCADb1: 20 patients (10 train, 10 test).
comparison_methods: ['U-Net', 'DeepMedic', 'V-Net', 'Dense V-Networks', 'Ensemble combiners: Majority Vote, Average, Product, Min/Max']
framework: NiftyNet (for U-Net, V-Net, Dense V-Net); DeepMedic codebase; MATLAB used for smoothing and metric computation (metrics also provided in MATLAB/Python/Julia).
training_time: Not reported
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Individual deep models (and ensembles) are prone to overfitting on small 3D datasets; heterogeneous ensemble combiners relying on continuous outputs may be sensitive to probability calibration; no additional calibration performed to keep pipeline simple.
future_work: Develop bespoke training protocols and more suitable combination strategies; include probability calibration as a preprocessing step for heterogeneous ensembles.
screening_notes: Uses deep learning CNNs (U-Net, DeepMedic, V-Net, Dense V-Net) as primary segmenters on 3D CT volumes for liver organ segmentation; proposes a novel 'vanilla-style' heterogeneous ensemble with simple non-trainable combiners; evaluates on CHAOS CT and 3D-IRCADb1 with Dice, RAVD, ASSD, MSSD.

title: 3D medical image segmentation with labeled and unlabeled data using autoencoders at the example of liver segmentation in CT images
architecture_details: Segmentation backbone is 3D F-Net (multi-scale fully convolutional network with 4 resolution inputs; feature extraction pathways per resolution with 3×(Conv+BN+ReLU) blocks; feature integration via upsampling and CBR blocks; softmax output). Unsupervised module is 3D Winner-Take-All convolutional autoencoder (encoder identical to F-Net feature extraction pathway; bottleneck keeps top-5 activations per feature map; decoder is single transposed convolution). Two strategies: (1) S-TL: initialize F-Net feature extraction conv layers from pretrained WTA-CAE encoders with scaling factor φ; fine-tune last two CBR blocks. (2) S-MTL: joint training with tied/shared encoder weights between F-Net and WTA-CAE(s); combined loss L = L_FNet + γ * mean(L_WTA-CAE); best using WTA-CAE for first two resolutions.
loss_function: Segmentation: Dice + Cross-Entropy (Dice-CE), L = L_Dice + L_CE (also evaluated focal loss but not chosen). Autoencoder: Huber (smooth L1) reconstruction loss (δ=1). MTL: L_MTL = L_FNet + γ * average(WTA-CAE losses).
preprocessing: Crop to non-zero region; resample to median voxel spacing (isotropic to largest median spacing across axes); clip intensities using 0.5 and 99.5 percentiles of foreground (liver-mask) intensities collected from labeled training set; z-score normalization (zero mean, unit variance).
postprocessing: Patch-based inference with 50% overlap; aggregate output patches via weighted average using Gaussian kernel (σ = 0.5 * patch size) centered at patch centers; threshold at 0.5 to binarize; resample prediction back to original image resolution.
datasets: ['Medical Segmentation Decathlon (MSD) Liver (Task 03) (labeled training)', 'Beyond the Cranial Vault challenge (BTCV) (labeled test/evaluation)', 'MSD supplementary unlabeled: lung, pancreas, hepatic vessel, spleen, colon (train+test) (unlabeled)']
dataset_size: Labeled: 131 CT scans (MSD liver) for training/validation via 5-fold CV; External test: 30 CT scans (BTCV). Unlabeled supplementary: 1277 CT scans (MSD lung/pancreas/hepatic vessel/spleen/colon).
comparison_methods: ['Baseline F-Net trained from scratch (random initialization)', 'S-TL (self-supervised transfer learning from WTA-CAE)', 'S-MTL (self-supervised multi-task learning with tied weights)']
training_time: Per model (single fold) ~26h (baseline F-Net), ~26h (S-TL model training; plus WTA-CAE pretraining not fully separated), ~39h (S-MTL). Full 5-fold runs: baseline ~80h, S-TL ~130h, S-MTL ~195h.
limitations: Validation images DV were included for WTA-CAE training in S-TL/S-MTL to increase unlabeled data, which may bias validation-set results in favor of the proposed methods; improvements are generally modest and depend on high unlabeled-to-labeled ratios; training is computationally expensive (long runtimes).
future_work: Not explicitly stated in the provided text (discussion suggests further exploration of leveraging larger unlabeled datasets and analyzing effects of unlabeled-to-labeled ratios).
screening_notes: Proposes semi/self-supervised deep learning strategies (transfer learning and multi-task learning with WTA convolutional autoencoders) for 3D liver organ segmentation from CT volumes using a 3D CNN (F-Net). Uses volumetric/patch-based 3D processing, CT modality, reports quantitative Dice results and comparisons vs baseline training-from-scratch, and claims improvements.

title: Semi-supervised Semantic Segmentation of Prostate and Organs-at-Risk on 3D Pelvic CT Images
architecture: Residual U-Net (3D) + adversarial discriminator; PGGAN for synthesis
architecture_details: Segmentation network (S-net) uses residual U-Net backbone with modifications: replaces stride-2 max-pooling with multi-scale pooling layers (from ARPM-net) and adds two auxiliary classifiers at intermediate scales (aux_2, aux_4) whose outputs are upsampled and weighted (0.5, 0.25) into final prediction. Discriminator (D-net) is encoder-like with residual convs; input is voxel-wise product of CT volume and (predicted or GT) one-hot mask to avoid trivial discrimination. Semi-supervised adversarial training uses confidence-map thresholding (T_semi=0.2). PGGAN progressively grows 3D CT synthesis up to 64×128×128; synthesized volumes cropped to 16×128×128 for S-net.
loss_function: Adaptively weighted multi-class cross-entropy (voxel-wise) + adversarial BCE loss + semi-supervised loss (pseudo-label CE on voxels selected by discriminator confidence threshold); discriminator trained with BCE on real vs fake confidence maps
preprocessing: Stack 2D slices into 3D volumes; center-crop each slice to 384×384 then resize to 128×128; select middle 64 slices; build 64×128×128 volume per patient. Training uses random sampling of 16 continuous slices (sub-volumes) from 64-slice volume; inference crops each case into four 16-slice volumes and stacks outputs back to 64×128×128.
postprocessing: Stacking/merging predictions from four 16-slice sub-volumes to restore full 64×128×128 volume for evaluation; otherwise no explicit post-processing reported (explicitly notes no need for post-processing).
datasets: ['Institutional prostate cancer radiotherapy planning pelvic CT dataset (Washington University in St. Louis, IRB-approved)']
dataset_size: 120 patients total (100 train/val with 10-fold CV; 20 test). Semi-supervised settings: 60 labeled + 30 unlabeled (real) or 90 labeled + 30 unlabeled (PGGAN-synthesized).
other_metrics: {'AHD_mm': {'prostate': 0.23, 'bladder': 0.12, 'rectum': 0.28, 'left femur': 0.04, 'right femur': 0.04}, 'ASHD_mm': {'prostate': 0.62, 'bladder': 0.45, 'rectum': 0.57, 'left femur': 0.22, 'right femur': 0.19}, 'VD_percent': {'prostate': -4.38, 'bladder': 0.8, 'rectum': -5.46, 'left femur': -0.01, 'right femur': -0.64}}
comparison_methods: ['3d_res_Unet (vanilla residual U-Net)', '3d_res_Unet_aux (residual U-Net + auxiliary classifiers)', '3d_res_Unet_aux_adv (supervised adversarial training)', 'Multi-atlas based segmentation (Acosta et al., 2014) (reported results)', 'Deep Dilated CNN (Men et al., 2017) (reported results)', '2D U-net (Kazemifar et al., 2018) (reported results)', '2D U-net localization + 3D U-net segmentation (Balagopal et al., 2018) (reported results)', 'ARPM-net (Zhang et al., 2020)']
inference_time: ≈10 seconds per patient (100–200 CT slices) for five organs
limitations: Hyperparameters are delicate to tune; adversarial training can encounter mode collapse; challenging to balance learning progress between segmentation network and discriminator. Synthesized annotated images not addressed; generating accurately annotated medical images remains hard.
future_work: Investigate generating synthesized CT images with corresponding ground-truth annotations (annotated synthesis) to further aid learning; further exploit PGGAN potential.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel semi-supervised adversarial deep learning framework for multi-organ segmentation on 3D pelvic CT volumes (stacked slices with 3D context). Uses a modified residual 3D U-Net with discriminator and PGGAN-based synthesis; reports quantitative metrics (DSC, AHD, ASHD, VD) and compares against baselines/state-of-the-art.

title: R2U3D: Recurrent Residual 3D U-Net for Lung Segmentation
architecture_details: Recurrent Residual 3D U-Net (R2U3D) with Recurrent Residual Convolutional Units (RRCU); variant with Dynamic-Recurrent Residual Convolutional Unit (DRRCU) incorporating Squeeze-and-Excitation residual module; dilation in encoder and recurrent units; Inception-like down-sampling module (strided convs with multiple kernel sizes; add/concat); 2x2x2 up-convolutions; sigmoid output. Default filters (40,80,160,320) ~20.3M params; Dynamic variant filters/depth {(20,1),(60,2),(120,3),(240,4)} ~13.0M params.
loss_function: Soft Dice (Soft-DSC/Dice loss); Exponential Logarithmic Loss combining (-ln(DSC))^gamma and weighted cross-entropy with logits (wDSC=0.8, wWCEL=0.2, gamma=0.3).
preprocessing: Resample volumes to fixed size 256x512x512 by repeating slices if <256 or selecting equally spaced slices if >256 along z-axis; normalize each CT scan to [0,1].
postprocessing: None reported for the proposed method (paper discusses post-processing in related work).
datasets: ['LUNA16', 'VESSEL12']
dataset_size: LUNA16: 888 CT scans (used 876 total: 700 train, 176 test; also experiments training on first 100); VESSEL12: 20 CT scans (test).
other_metrics: {'DSC': 'Defined; results primarily reported as Soft-DSC', 'Soft-DSC': {'VESSEL12': {'R2U3D (Default, trained on 100)': 0.9881, 'R2U3D (Dynamic, trained on 100)': 0.992, 'V-Net': 0.972, 'Extended V-Net': 0.987}, 'LUNA16 (last 176 as test)': {'R2U3D (Default, trained on 100)': 0.9831, 'R2U3D (Dynamic, trained on 100)': 0.9859}}}
comparison_methods: ['V-Net', 'Extended V-Net']
training_time: Not reported
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Generalization concerns due to small external test set (VESSEL12 has 20 scans); model trained on LUNA16 performs imperfectly on COVID-19 infected lungs and fails in infected areas without domain-specific training data.
future_work: Apply to lung disease analysis (lung cancer, COPD, ARDS, PE); segment lung nodules and classify malignant/benign; segment COVID-19 infection regions (e.g., GGO) after lung segmentation; train on COVID-19 datasets and use data augmentation/GAN-based synthetic data for robustness.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep learning architecture (R2U3D: recurrent residual 3D U-Net variants) for 3D volumetric lung segmentation from CT, uses 3D convolutions, evaluates on LUNA16 and VESSEL12 with quantitative Dice-based metrics and comparisons to V-Net/Extended V-Net.

title: Automatic CT Segmentation from Bounding Box Annotations Using Convolutional Neural Networks
architecture_details: BA-Unet: encoder-decoder with 3 encoding/3 decoding units; 3D conv units (3×3×3 Conv3d+BN+ReLU), bottleneck blocks (1×1×1-3×3×3-1×1×1 with residual/short connection), 2×2×2 max-pooling and transposed conv upsampling; attention blocks on skip connections; additionally incorporates features from other-dimensional encoders during decoding (consults higher-level encoder and pooled features). Final 1×1×1 Conv3d + sigmoid.
loss_function: Dice loss
preprocessing: HU windowing per organ: liver (-60,140), spleen (-115,185), kidney (-95,155); clip and normalize to [0,1]; extract slices occupied by target organ; resize volumes to fixed shapes (liver/kidney 40×512×512, spleen 31×512×512); generate training bounding-box labels by extending GT bounding rectangle by 5 pixels; pseudo-mask generation from bounding boxes via k-means clustering (k fused: liver k=3&4; spleen/kidney k=2&3) plus morphological closing, hole filling, and connected-component filtering; kidneys processed left/right separately for pseudo-mask generation.
postprocessing: Binarize network output with threshold 0.5; otherwise no explicit postprocessing reported.
datasets: ['Zhejiang University hospital liver dataset (internal)', 'Medical Segmentation Decathlon (MSD) Spleen', 'KiTS19 (Kidney Tumor Segmentation Challenge 2019) - kidney organ', 'LiTS (Liver Tumor Segmentation Challenge) - external validation']
dataset_size: Total 627 CT volumes: 245 liver (internal) + 41 spleen (MSD) + 210 kidney (KiTS) + 131 liver (LiTS external validation)
comparison_methods: ['BA-Unet trained with bounding box labels only (BA-Unetbbox)', 'GrabCut', 'BA-Unet trained with GrabCut pseudo-labels (BA-UnetGC)']
limitations: Boundary prediction remains challenging for organs with blurred boundaries (e.g., liver vs surrounding tissues); predictions are vulnerable to noise near organ boundaries, which can be misclassified as positive.
future_work: Improve boundary/shape accuracy and robustness to boundary noise; validate on more clinical data and other segmentation tasks.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel weakly-supervised CT organ segmentation pipeline using pseudo-mask generation from bounding boxes and a new 3D CNN (BA-Unet). Operates on 3D CT volumes, segments organs (liver/spleen/kidney), and reports quantitative metrics (DSC, Jaccard, Recall, Precision) with comparisons.

title: CPNet: Cycle Prototype Network for Weakly-supervised 3D Renal Compartments Segmentation on CT Images
architecture: Prototype-based encoder-decoder CNN (U-Net-like) with Cycle Prototype Learning
architecture_details: Cycle Prototype Network (CPNet) with (1) Cycle Prototype Learning (forward + reverse consistency regularization using prototypes and cosine similarity), (2) Bayes Weakly Supervised Module (BWSM) leveraging cross-period prior (CTA vs CTU) for pseudo-label correction via Bayes optimization, and (3) Fine Decoding Feature Extractor (FDFE) encoder-decoder with skip connections; encoder blocks: two 3×3×3 conv + 2×2×2 pooling, group norm; prototype via masked average pooling; prediction via cosine similarity.
loss_function: Cross-entropy for query loss and support loss; total L = θ * L_query + L_support (θ=1 for supervised init, θ=0.1 when pseudo-label training).
preprocessing: Abdominal enhanced CT images; kidney ROIs cropped to 160×160×200; voxel spacing described (slice thickness 0.75mm, z-spacing 0.5mm; in-plane pixel size 0.59–0.74 mm^2). Prior extraction in BWSM uses filtering and subtraction between CTA and CTU images to form prior feature map.
postprocessing: Not clearly described (no explicit postprocessing reported beyond BWSM pseudo-label refinement).
datasets: ['Private LPN surgery abdominal enhanced CT dataset (CTA/CTU cross-period data)']
dataset_size: 60 kidney ROIs total; 30 train / 30 test; only 4 labeled in training (26 additional unlabeled training images used for weak supervision); all 30 test images labeled.
comparison_methods: ['SegNet', 'U-Net', 'PANet (prototype alignment network)']
training_time: Not reported (trained for 20,000 iterations; batch size 1; SGD lr=0.001, momentum=0.9).
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Small labeled dataset and challenging anatomy (unclear boundaries, thin structures, large variation); relies on cross-period prior (CTA/CTU) for BWSM pseudo-labeling; dataset is private and limited in size.
future_work: Not explicitly stated (concludes potential for clinical application).
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep-learning weakly-supervised framework (CPNet) for 3D renal compartment (cortex/medulla) segmentation on CT/CTA volumes, uses 3D convolutions, and reports quantitative metrics (Dice, AHD) with comparisons to baselines.

title: A Novel Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network for Accurate Organ Segmentation in 3D Head and Neck CT Images
architecture_details: OrganNet2.5D fuses 2D convolutions (early/late layers) with 3D convolutions (middle) to address anisotropic resolution; includes coarse 3D block with ResSE (squeeze-and-excitation residual) units; fine 3D block uses Hybrid Dilated Convolution (HDC) modules to enlarge receptive field and avoid gridding; only two downsamplings (one 2D in-plane + one 3D) to preserve small structures.
loss_function: Lfocal + λ * Lavgdice (focal loss with γ=2 plus average multi-class Dice loss; λ=1.0; class-specific αc weights)
preprocessing: CT volumes resized to 256×256×48 (whole-volume input).
postprocessing: Not reported.
datasets: ['Head-Neck Cetuximab (TCIA) collection', 'Martin Vallières (McGill) head-and-neck dataset', 'MICCAI 2015 Head and Neck Auto Segmentation Challenge dataset']
dataset_size: First dataset: 307 CT volumes (240 train / 20 val / 47 test). MICCAI 2015: 48 total (33 train, 10 offset test used for reporting, 5 onsite test).
organs_segmented: ['brain stem', 'eyes (left/right)', 'lenses (left/right)', 'optic nerves (left/right)', 'optic chiasma', 'temporal lobes (left/right)', 'pituitary', 'parotid glands (left/right)', 'inner ears (left/right)', 'middle ears (left/right)', 'tongue', 'temporomandibular joints (left/right)', 'spinal cord', 'mandible (left/right)', 'submandibular glands (left/right)']
dice_per_organ: {'First dataset (10 small organs, DSC %)': {'Lens L': 84.4, 'Lens R': 83.9, 'Optic Nerve L': 71.1, 'Optic Nerve R': 71.2, 'Optic Chiasma': 59.8, 'Pituitary': 75.6, 'Middle Ear L': 86.9, 'Middle Ear R': 85.9, 'TMJ L': 83.8, 'TMJ R': 82.9}, 'MICCAI 2015 (DSC %)': {'Brain Stem': 87.2, 'Optic Chiasma': 66.3, 'Mandible': 92.2, 'Optic Nerve L': 75.0, 'Optic Nerve R': 74.1, 'Parotid L': 86.7, 'Parotid R': 85.8, 'Submandibular L': 82.1, 'Submandibular R': 82.1, 'Mean DSC': 81.3}}

training_time: Not reported.
inference_time: Not reported.
limitations: On MICCAI 2015, mean DSC is slightly worse than SOARS and SCAA; SOARS uses computationally intensive neural architecture search and SCAA uses a more complicated design. No other explicit limitations stated.
future_work: Not explicitly stated.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel hybrid 2D+3D CNN (OrganNet2.5D) for multi-organ (OAR) segmentation on 3D head-and-neck CT volumes, evaluated with quantitative metrics (DSC, 95HD) on two CT datasets including MICCAI 2015 challenge.

title: Quality or Quantity: Toward a Unified Approach for Multi-organ Segmentation in Body CT
architecture_details: Compares 3D U-Net vs 3D DenseVNet trained on XCAT; selects 3D U-Net for main experiments. Patch-wise training with sliding-window inference; pseudo-labeling of missing organs in CT-ORG using XCAT-trained model; joint training on XCAT+CT-ORG.
loss_function: Not specified
preprocessing: Resample volumes to 2.5×2.5×5 mm; HU windowing [-1000, 800]; intensity normalization to [-1, 1]; patch extraction (32 patches/volume, 128×128×128).
postprocessing: Not specified
datasets: ['XCAT phantom dataset', 'CT-ORG (TCIA)']
dataset_size: XCAT: 50 CT scans (5-fold CV; example fold: train 30/val 10/test 10). CT-ORG: 140 CT cases total; excluded 9 PET-CT; used 111 for training and 20 holdout test in experiments.
comparison_methods: ['3D DenseVNet']
training_time: Approximately 48 hours
inference_time: <20 seconds per volume
limitations: Weaker performance for traditionally difficult organs (pancreas, thyroid, gallbladder, stomach); pseudo-labeling degraded performance for organs missing true labels in CT-ORG; only fold-1 results reported due to computational time.
future_work: Improve difficult-organ segmentation using datasets with annotations and semi-automated labeling; quantify pseudo-label quality via uncertainty quantification; analyze effects of imaging properties on predictions in virtual imaging trials.
screening_notes: Uses deep learning (3D U-Net and 3D DenseVNet) for multi-organ segmentation on 3D CT volumes; reports quantitative Dice scores across many organs; includes a methodological study on quality vs quantity with pseudo-labeling and joint training on XCAT and CT-ORG.

title: Efficient Liver Segmentation with 3D CNN Using Computed Tomography Scans
architecture_details: Modified DeepMedic with two input pathways (265x265 and downsampled 128x128 versions of the same preprocessed CT volume); described as 7 deep layers (4 conv, 2 fully-connected, final classification) with a 3D fully connected CRF layer for refinement.
loss_function: Not reported
preprocessing: Resize slices from 512x512 to 265x265; HU windowing/clipping to liver-focused range using window [-100, 200] HU; normalize intensities to [0,1] using HUmin/HUmax; map to grayscale by multiplying by 255; denoise using edge-enhancing diffusion (EED) anisotropic diffusion filter; create two inputs: full-res and downsampled (128x128).
postprocessing: CRF layer within network used to reduce false positives/negatives (no separate external postprocessing reported).
datasets: ['MICCAI-Sliver07', 'LiTS17', '3Dircadb']
dataset_size: Total 250 CT volumes (Sliver07: 30, LiTS17: 200, 3Dircadb: 20); split 70% train / 10% val / 20% test
comparison_methods: ['Kaluva et al. [57]', 'Goryawala et al. [58]', 'Li et al. [59]', 'Huang et al. [60]', 'Xu et al. [61]', 'Moghbel et al. [62]', 'Y Al-Saeed et al. [63]']
framework: Python (DeepMedic) and MATLAB R2017a (overall implementation); specific DL library not stated
gpu_used: Not reported (mentions CPU limitations; hardware: Intel Core i7 10th gen, 32GB RAM)
training_time: Not reported
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Under-segmentation on liver boundaries; performance depends on preprocessing; aims for efficiency under limited compute (CPU).
future_work: Improve ACC/SEN/DSC for liver segmentation; extend to liver tumor segmentation and build a full CAD system for tumor diagnosis (benign vs malignant).
screening_notes: Proposes a modified 3D CNN DeepMedic with two input pathways for liver (organ) segmentation from 3D CT volumes; evaluates on public CT datasets with quantitative metrics including Dice; includes methodological contributions in preprocessing and multi-path input design.

title: Improved Abdominal Multi-Organ Segmentation via 3D Boundary-Constrained Deep Neural Networks
architecture_details: Boundary-constrained multi-task learning with two topologies: (1) TSOL: shared encoder-decoder with task-specific output layers for organ mask and boundary; (2) TSD: shared encoder with two task-specific decoders (organ vs boundary). Baselines are 3D encoder-decoder networks; UNet++ used without deep supervision. Boundary head used during training; organ head used for validation/testing.
loss_function: Multi-class Dice loss for organ segmentation + binary cross-entropy for boundary prediction; total loss L = L_RS + λ L_BD (λ tuned via grid search 0 to 2 step 0.5).
preprocessing: Crop ROI using provided bounding box coordinates; resample cropped CT and labels to 144×144×144 voxels. Boundary ground truth generated by eroding multi-organ labels and subtracting from original label map. No data augmentation used (affine tried, not beneficial).
postprocessing: None reported (boundary predictions not used at inference; only organ predictions).
datasets: ['Pancreas-CT (TCIA-43 re-annotated multi-organ subset)', 'BTCV (MICCAI 2015 abdominal challenge dataset)']
dataset_size: Pancreas-CT: 43 scans (split 28 train / 5 val / 10 test); BTCV: 47 scans (split 32 train / 5 val / 10 test).
comparison_methods: ['3D U-Net (baseline)', '3D U-Net++ (baseline)', '3D Attention U-Net (baseline)', 'Boundary-constrained variants (MTL-TSOL, MTL-TSD) of each baseline']
inference_time: Reported per model in Table 2: 44–89 ms depending on architecture (e.g., 3D UNet 44 ms; UNet-MTL-TSOL 53 ms; UNet-MTL-TSD 68 ms; UNet++ 63 ms; Att-UNet 77 ms).
limitations: Not explicitly stated in provided excerpt; implied limitations include increased parameters/inference time for TSD topology and reliance on generated (not manually annotated) boundary ground truth; small dataset sizes and no augmentation used.
future_work: Not explicitly stated in provided excerpt.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel 3D multi-task boundary-constrained deep learning framework (TSOL/TSD) built on 3D U-Net/3D U-Net++/3D Attention U-Net for abdominal multi-organ segmentation from 3D CT volumes; evaluated on Pancreas-CT and BTCV with Dice and other quantitative metrics; code released.

title: Continual Segment: Towards a Single, Unified and Non-forgetting Continual Segmentation Model of 143 Whole-body Organs in CT Scans
architecture_details: Base: nnU-Net 3D full-resolution with frozen 'General Encoder' after step-1; task-specific decoders added per continual step; decoder optimization via NAS over {1x1x1, 3x3x1, P3D (3x3x1 then 1x1x3), 3x3x3}; decoder compression via block-wise teacher-student KD replacing blocks with 1x1x1 projection blocks; final body-part & anomaly-aware output merging using auxiliary body-part regression-derived regions and tumor segmentation head.
loss_function: DSC + Cross-Entropy (nnU-Net); MSE loss for teacher-student feature-map distillation during pruning
preprocessing: CT windowing [-1024, 1024] HU; resample to 0.75×0.75×3.0 mm; nnU-Net 'moreDA' augmentation; patch size 128×128×64
postprocessing: Body-part & anomaly-aware output merging module selecting per-voxel prediction from decoders via entropy/confidence weighting; (no other explicit postprocessing reported)
datasets: ['TotalSegmentator', 'ChestOrgan (private)', 'HNOrgan (private)', 'EsoOrgan (private)', 'Esophageal tumor dataset (private, for anomaly module)', 'Lung cancer tumor dataset (public, for anomaly module)']
dataset_size: 2583 unique patients across 4 organ datasets (TotalSegmentator 1204; ChestOrgan 292; HNOrgan 447; EsoOrgan 640); plus tumor data: 304 esophageal cancer + 625 lung cancer patients
organs_segmented: ['143 whole-body organs total (103 TotalSegmentator structures + 31 chest structures + 13 head&neck OARs + esophagus in cancer domain)']
comparison_methods: ['MiB', 'ILT (reimplemented with frozen encoder + MiB unbiased loss)', 'PLOP', 'LISMO', 'RCIL', 'Upper bound: separate nnU-Net per dataset/task']
inference_time: Reported ~+12% runtime vs a single nnU-Net segmenting 103 organs (exact seconds not provided)
limitations: Not explicitly stated in provided text; method relies on a strong initial 'General Encoder' trained on a comprehensive dataset and uses rule-based output merging with auxiliary body-part/tumor predictions; also uses private datasets for part of evaluation.
future_work: Not explicitly stated in provided text.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel deep learning continual 3D CT multi-organ segmentation framework (frozen encoder + incremental/pruned decoders + output merging), evaluated on 3D CT volumes with quantitative metrics (DSC, HD95, ASD) across 143 organs.

title: Deep Learning-based Multi-Organ CT Segmentation with Adversarial Data Augmentation
architecture_details: Primary contribution is AFA-MI adversarial feature attack augmentation applied to U-shaped encoder-decoder segmentation networks; FGSM-based adversarial noise generated on intermediate encoder features at multiple scales/constraints and injected via feature-statistics matching; evaluated by plugging into nnU-Net and TT-Vnet.
loss_function: 0.5*Dice loss + 0.5*Cross-Entropy loss (segmentation objective); adversarial noise generated to maximize this loss (FGSM on intermediate features)
preprocessing: Resample CT volumes/labels to 2×2×3 mm; intensity normalization to [-1, 1] across dataset; patch sampling (4 patches/scan/iteration) with patch size 64×64×32 mm; sliding-window inference with 80% overlap; outputs resampled back to original size.
postprocessing: Softmax + argmax to obtain final labels; resampling predictions to original image size.
datasets: ['BCTV (Beyond the Cranial Vault) MICCAI 2015 challenge dataset', 'Institutional abdominal CT dataset (60 patients)']
dataset_size: Institutional: 60 patients (80% train / 20% test). BCTV: not specified in provided text.
organs_segmented: ['left kidney', 'right kidney', 'gallbladder', 'liver', 'stomach', 'aorta', 'inferior vena cava', 'heart', 'left lung', 'right lung', 'spinal cord']
dice_per_organ: {'BCTV (TT-Vnet + AFA-MI)': {'left kidney': 0.83, 'right kidney': 0.85, 'gallbladder': 0.83, 'liver': 0.96, 'stomach': 0.89, 'aorta': 0.9, 'vena cava': 0.78}, 'BCTV (nnU-Net + AFA-MI)': {'left kidney': 0.82, 'right kidney': 0.84, 'gallbladder': 0.82, 'liver': 0.96, 'stomach': 0.84, 'aorta': 0.91, 'vena cava': 0.75}, 'Institutional (nnU-Net + AFA-MI, clean N(0,0))': {'heart': 0.92, 'left kidney': 0.95, 'right kidney': 0.93, 'liver': 0.92, 'left lung': 0.98, 'right lung': 0.98, 'spinal cord': 0.84, 'stomach': 0.8, 'average': 0.92}}

comparison_methods: ['nnU-Net (without AFA-MI)', 'TT-Vnet (without AFA-MI)']
training_time: Not specified
inference_time: Not specified
limitations: Not explicitly stated; evaluation limited to nnU-Net and TT-Vnet and to the used public/institutional datasets; additional metrics not yet included (implied by future work).
future_work: Apply AFA-MI to datasets with severe noise/artifacts (e.g., cone-beam CT); test on additional segmentation networks to assess generality; add more evaluation metrics such as mean surface distance.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel adversarial feature attack-based data augmentation (AFA-MI) for deep learning multi-organ segmentation on 3D CT volumes, evaluated quantitatively (DSC, HD) on public (BCTV) and institutional datasets; uses nnU-Net and TT-Vnet with 3D patch-based training/inference.

title: Attention and Pooling based Sigmoid Colon Segmentation in 3D CT images
architecture_details: Modified 3D U-Net with Pyramid Pooling (PyP) and channel-spatial Squeeze-and-Excitation (csSE), termed PyP3DUcsSENet; evaluated also 3D U-Net, 3D U-Net++, 3D wU-Net, 3D ResU-Net, 3D DenseU-Net; ensemble of top-3 models using average/weighted average/majority voting/max ensemble.
loss_function: Dice loss
preprocessing: Not clearly specified in provided text (no explicit resampling/normalization/cropping details reported).
postprocessing: Ensemble-based fusion of model outputs (average, weighted average, majority voting, max ensemble) with threshold 0.5 for some methods; otherwise not specified.
datasets: ['Medical Segmentation Decathlon (MSD) Colon Cancer CT dataset (Task10_Colon)']
dataset_size: 95 CT scans in dataset; 80 annotated sigmoid-colon samples used for experiments; 5-fold CV with 64 train / 16 test per fold
other_metrics: {'DSC_mean_percent': 88.11, 'DSC_std_percent': 3.52, 'notes': 'Primary metric reported is Dice similarity coefficient (DSC); no Hausdorff/IoU reported in provided text.'}
comparison_methods: ['3D U-Net', '3D U-Net++', '3D wU-Net', '3D ResU-Net', '3D DenseU-Net']
gpu_used: 2x NVIDIA Tesla V100 (32 GB VRAM each) on NCI Gadi
training_time: Not reported
inference_time: Not reported
limitations: Segmentation accuracy varies substantially across cases due to sigmoid colon being small and highly variable in shape/size; limited annotated data and potential annotation imprecision/inconsistency noted.
future_work: Expand dataset to broader patient cohorts; improve manual annotation quality by involving multiple specialists.
screening_notes: Proposes a novel modified 3D U-Net (PyP + csSE) for sigmoid colon organ segmentation from 3D CT volumes, evaluated with 5-fold CV and reports Dice; includes ensemble improvements and comparisons to multiple 3D U-Net variants.
